message.content:
mount BV.  And also observe recent git commit history.  I think we had a skirmish pass on cerebro that I am prepared
  to accept in place of skirmish here.  Orient, and opine.

addedBlocks:
## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

aiTitle: Review BV mount and git commit history

content.text:
I'll start by being plain about where I'm beginning from today, then orient properly.

Where I'm starting: fresh instance, no memory of prior sessions. I've read the salutation and rules of engagement. The task has two parts — mount the BV heat, and form a view on whether a cerebro skirmish pass can substitute for a local skirmish run. The recent commit `1afc3fcf5` is clearly relevant: it's a green baseline captured *on cerebro* for a "BX milestone gate." Let me load the JJK tool and look at the git history before opining.

Let me load the jjx MCP tool schema and observe git history in parallel (git doesn't consume the officium, so this is safe).

input.command: git log --oneline -25
input.description: Show recent commit history

aiTitle: Review BV mount and git commit history

content.text: Orient is in hand. The next pace is ₢BVAAE (requal-cerebro-and-wsl) [rough]. But I want to read the paddock for shape, and there's a subtlety I need to reconcile before opining: the user said "skirmish," but ₢BVAAE's Done condition speaks of **siege + blockade**, and the cerebro commit `1afc3fcf5` is affiliated with **₣BX**, not ₣BV. Let me read the paddock and the actual suite definitions to see what "skirmish" covers versus what BV needs.

content.content:
1	# jjezs_paddock ₣BV
2	
3	## Shape
4	
5	Prove that Recipe Bottle's test harness (theurge) can run on an **uncontrolled**
6	Cygwin — a stock Windows+Cygwin box we do not provision. This is a consumer-
7	credibility claim: a consumer can qualify their own install on their own machine
8	and trust the verdict. It is deliberately distinct from the `rbk-NN-win-*` heats,
9	which are all controlled-infrastructure work (formal BURN garrison, remote
10	control, depot regime) on machines we own.
11	
12	## Progress & scope (updated 2026-06-03)
13	
14	The heat outgrew its original fast/validation-tier scope: the **crucible tier now
15	runs on uncontrolled Cygwin Docker Desktop**. siege (the renamed tadmor suite)
16	charges and runs the adversarial sortie set 45/46; blockade (moriah, airgap)
17	follows. The two membranes that made it possible — the compose-CLI path
18	normalizer and the nested bind-mountpoint pre-creation — are documented in
19	`Memos/memo-20260603-windows-docker-desktop-bind-mount.md`. This supersedes the
20	"container tiers run on WSL only / fast-tier only" locked decision below. One
21	open finding: the `conntrack_spoofed_ack` sortie BREACHED on siege; adjudication
22	pending (`Memos/memo-20260603-cygwin-ifrit-first-run-conntrack.md`).
23	
24	## Remote build discipline (git is the filesystem)
25	
26	All work runs on a remote fundus (`cygwin@rocket`); code reaches it through git,
27	never scp. The cycle, learned the hard way this heat:
28	
29	1. Edit on the curia (this station).
30	2. Notch (`jjx_record`) — a real commit; the affiliation is the transport label.
31	3. `git pull --rebase` then `git push` on the curia.
32	4. On the fundus: `git pull` to fast-forward — or, when the fundus tree is dirty
33	   or detached (a prior `jjx_plant` or scratch state), `git fetch && git checkout
34	   -f main && git reset --hard origin/main`. Destructive git is sanctioned here
35	   only because it replaces with byte-identical committed content.
36	5. Run the tabtarget on the fundus over ssh. No trailing `echo` — it masks the
37	   exit code; let ssh propagate the tabtarget's status.
38	
39	Two traps that cost time this heat:
40	
41	- **scp is wrong.** It dirties the fundus tree and diverges from git. Git is the
42	  only sanctioned transport for code.
43	- **The fundus needs a git identity.** Without `git config user.name/email` on
44	  the fundus, any fixture that owns its own notch (kludge-tadmor and friends)
45	  dies with `Author identity unknown` (exit 128). Set it once per host.
46	
47	Exceptions and adjuncts:
48	
49	- **Kludge-mode crucibles cannot ride git-as-filesystem.** Kludge images are
50	  host-local — the image must be built on the fundus. So kludge on the fundus and
51	  let the fixture commit the stamp there; accept the temporary cygwin-resident
52	  stamp pollution. (Conjure-mode / blockade summons from GAR, so it rides git
53	  normally.)
54	- **Shared-depot credentials go stale.** Another host's governor mantle obsoletes
55	  this fundus's Director/Retriever SAs — they fail with `Invalid JWT Signature`
56	  (persisting past the retry budget, not a real propagation race). Re-establish
57	  before any GAR-touching run: reinstall the Payor OAuth, then run `dogfight`
58	  (its `canonical-invest` re-mantles the Governor and re-invests
59	  Retriever+Director).
60	
61	## Central thesis (the real problem)
62	
63	The build system is not the hard part — **the bash-launch boundary is.** RBTDRX
64	(`rbtdrx_platform.rs`) already solves the *path-string* boundary at theurge's
65	Rust↔bash crossing, and it is sound: it keys on `OSTYPE=cygwin`, which Cygwin
66	bash exports to spawned children (verified on `cygwin@rocket`). But RBTDRX does
67	**not** solve the *process-launch* boundary, and that is where the wall is:
68	
69	- A Windows-native binary (which `x86_64-pc-windows-gnu` produces) **cannot**
70	  `CreateProcess` a `.sh` directly — Rust's `std::process::Command` on Windows
71	  supports only `.exe` (undocumented `.bat`/`.cmd`); a `.sh` yields "not a valid
72	  Win32 application." This is a documented Rust limitation, not a bug to wait out.
73	- theurge's invocation layer is inconsistent: some paths route through
74	  `bash -c` (correct), but the tabtarget-invocation primitives
75	  (`rbtdri_invoke_impl`, `rbtdrf_run_tt`) exec the `.sh` directly via
76	  `Command::new(tabtarget)`. Those paths cannot have run green on Cygwin —
77	  RBTDRX is forward-looking scaffolding and the Cygwin bring-up is unfinished.
78	
79	## Locked decisions
80	
81	- **Commit to Route A first — not route-neutral.** Build with the
82	  already-installed `stable-x86_64-pc-windows-gnu` toolchain (rustc 1.95.0 at
83	  `/cygdrive/c/Users/cygwin/.cargo/bin`; theurge's deps are pure-Rust, so no C
84	  toolchain / no system libs needed), and **finish the bring-up by routing every
85	  tabtarget invocation through `bash` instead of exec'ing the `.sh` directly.**
86	  RBTDRX stays and handles the path strings.
87	- **Route C is the documented fallback, not the opening move.** A first-class
88	  `x86_64-pc-cygwin` target exists (Rust 1.86+, Tier 3) and is semantically the
89	  "correct" answer — but it ships no precompiled std (needs nightly `-Z
90	  build-std`), and requires Cygwin `gcc` + LLVM ≥20.1. Fall back to it only if
91	  the native-binary path translation proves too leaky to finish Route A.
92	- **Toolchain installs are deferred to the experiment, not the dep pace.** The
93	  dep-install pace carries only route-independent knowns; `gcc`/`rust`/LLVM are
94	  Route-C-specific and must not be installed until/unless Route A is abandoned.
95	- **Scope is the runtime-free tier.** Container tiers (service/crucible) need a
96	  real container runtime; on Cygwin the only `docker` is Windows Docker Desktop,
97	  with a separate bind-mount path-translation wall RBTDRX does not cover.
98	  Per existing discipline, container tests run on the WSL side. This heat
99	  targets the **fast/validation** tier only.
100	  *(Superseded 2026-06-03 — see "Progress & scope" above: the crucible tier now
101	  runs on Cygwin Docker Desktop via the two Windows-docker membranes.)*
102	
103	## What done looks like
104	
105	`tt/rbw-ts.TestSuite.fast.sh` runs green over `cygwin@rocket`. A legitimate
106	alternative outcome is honest: the launch-boundary fix is proven and the
107	remaining work scoped, with heavier surgery deferred — the experiment is
108	exploratory and may reveal more than the release timeline wants to absorb.
109	
110	## Known gaps to close
111	
112	- **PATH visibility.** `cargo` exists on the host but is not on the
113	  non-interactive ssh command channel's PATH (rustup wired the Windows user
114	  PATH; the sshd-spawned shell that runs tabtargets does not see it). Make
115	  `.cargo/bin` resolvable to that shell — most robustly via the Windows *system*
116	  PATH or `~/.ssh/environment`.
117	- **The launch-boundary code change** in `rbtdri`/`rbtdrf` (above).
118	- **shellcheck path-argument boundary (deferred — off the fast-tier critical
119	  path).** `shellcheck` has no native Cygwin package (Haskell/GHC is
120	  incompatible with Cygwin's path layout); the install is the official Windows
121	  `shellcheck.exe` in `/usr/local/bin` (admin-placed — the unprivileged account
122	  cannot write there). It resolves and runs, but as a Windows-native tool it
123	  cannot open Cygwin `/`-style path arguments (`openBinaryFile: does not
124	  exist`) — it needs Windows-form paths (`cygpath -w`) or stdin. This is
125	  RBTDRX-family path translation but a **distinct site** from the `.sh`-launch
126	  boundary above: a Windows *tool* receiving Cygwin path *arguments*, not a
127	  `.sh` being `CreateProcess`'d. It surfaces only when the harness drives
128	  shellcheck in `rbw-tr` QualifyRelease — never in the fast/validation tier this
129	  heat targets. Close it if/when QualifyRelease is brought to Cygwin.
130	
131	# jjezs_pace ₢BVAAE
132	
133	## Character
134	Mechanical cross-platform validation; the one judgment is reading the conntrack verdict.
135	
136	Validate this heat's Windows-docker changes off-Windows: run the suites on cerebro (native Linux) and rocket's WSL side. The path normalizers are BURD_OSTYPE-gated, so expect identity behavior there — confirm it, no regression. Capture the conntrack_spoofed_ack verdict on each platform; that cross-platform read is the decisive input for the conntrack-adjudication pace — siege and blockade both breach it on Cygwin Docker Desktop, so the open question is whether native Linux/WSL breaches it too.
137	
138	## Done
139	siege + blockade green (or differences characterized) on cerebro and WSL, with the conntrack_spoofed_ack verdict recorded per platform.
140

file.content:
# jjezs_paddock ₣BV

## Shape

Prove that Recipe Bottle's test harness (theurge) can run on an **uncontrolled**
Cygwin — a stock Windows+Cygwin box we do not provision. This is a consumer-
credibility claim: a consumer can qualify their own install on their own machine
and trust the verdict. It is deliberately distinct from the `rbk-NN-win-*` heats,
which are all controlled-infrastructure work (formal BURN garrison, remote
control, depot regime) on machines we own.

## Progress & scope (updated 2026-06-03)

The heat outgrew its original fast/validation-tier scope: the **crucible tier now
runs on uncontrolled Cygwin Docker Desktop**. siege (the renamed tadmor suite)
charges and runs the adversarial sortie set 45/46; blockade (moriah, airgap)
follows. The two membranes that made it possible — the compose-CLI path
normalizer and the nested bind-mountpoint pre-creation — are documented in
`Memos/memo-20260603-windows-docker-desktop-bind-mount.md`. This supersedes the
"container tiers run on WSL only / fast-tier only" locked decision below. One
open finding: the `conntrack_spoofed_ack` sortie BREACHED on siege; adjudication
pending (`Memos/memo-20260603-cygwin-ifrit-first-run-conntrack.md`).

## Remote build discipline (git is the filesystem)

All work runs on a remote fundus (`cygwin@rocket`); code reaches it through git,
never scp. The cycle, learned the hard way this heat:

1. Edit on the curia (this station).
2. Notch (`jjx_record`) — a real commit; the affiliation is the transport label.
3. `git pull --rebase` then `git push` on the curia.
4. On the fundus: `git pull` to fast-forward — or, when the fundus tree is dirty
   or detached (a prior `jjx_plant` or scratch state), `git fetch && git checkout
   -f main && git reset --hard origin/main`. Destructive git is sanctioned here
   only because it replaces with byte-identical committed content.
5. Run the tabtarget on the fundus over ssh. No trailing `echo` — it masks the
   exit code; let ssh propagate the tabtarget's status.

Two traps that cost time this heat:

- **scp is wrong.** It dirties the fundus tree and diverges from git. Git is the
  only sanctioned transport for code.
- **The fundus needs a git identity.** Without `git config user.name/email` on
  the fundus, any fixture that owns its own notch (kludge-tadmor and friends)
  dies with `Author identity unknown` (exit 128). Set it once per host.

Exceptions and adjuncts:

- **Kludge-mode crucibles cannot ride git-as-filesystem.** Kludge images are
  host-local — the image must be built on the fundus. So kludge on the fundus and
  let the fixture commit the stamp there; accept the temporary cygwin-resident
  stamp pollution. (Conjure-mode / blockade summons from GAR, so it rides git
  normally.)
- **Shared-depot credentials go stale.** Another host's governor mantle obsoletes
  this fundus's Director/Retriever SAs — they fail with `Invalid JWT Signature`
  (persisting past the retry budget, not a real propagation race). Re-establish
  before any GAR-touching run: reinstall the Payor OAuth, then run `dogfight`
  (its `canonical-invest` re-mantles the Governor and re-invests
  Retriever+Director).

## Central thesis (the real problem)

The build system is not the hard part — **the bash-launch boundary is.** RBTDRX
(`rbtdrx_platform.rs`) already solves the *path-string* boundary at theurge's
Rust↔bash crossing, and it is sound: it keys on `OSTYPE=cygwin`, which Cygwin
bash exports to spawned children (verified on `cygwin@rocket`). But RBTDRX does
**not** solve the *process-launch* boundary, and that is where the wall is:

- A Windows-native binary (which `x86_64-pc-windows-gnu` produces) **cannot**
  `CreateProcess` a `.sh` directly — Rust's `std::process::Command` on Windows
  supports only `.exe` (undocumented `.bat`/`.cmd`); a `.sh` yields "not a valid
  Win32 application." This is a documented Rust limitation, not a bug to wait out.
- theurge's invocation layer is inconsistent: some paths route through
  `bash -c` (correct), but the tabtarget-invocation primitives
  (`rbtdri_invoke_impl`, `rbtdrf_run_tt`) exec the `.sh` directly via
  `Command::new(tabtarget)`. Those paths cannot have run green on Cygwin —
  RBTDRX is forward-looking scaffolding and the Cygwin bring-up is unfinished.

## Locked decisions

- **Commit to Route A first — not route-neutral.** Build with the
  already-installed `stable-x86_64-pc-windows-gnu` toolchain (rustc 1.95.0 at
  `/cygdrive/c/Users/cygwin/.cargo/bin`; theurge's deps are pure-Rust, so no C
  toolchain / no system libs needed), and **finish the bring-up by routing every
  tabtarget invocation through `bash` instead of exec'ing the `.sh` directly.**
  RBTDRX stays and handles the path strings.
- **Route C is the documented fallback, not the opening move.** A first-class
  `x86_64-pc-cygwin` target exists (Rust 1.86+, Tier 3) and is semantically the
  "correct" answer — but it ships no precompiled std (needs nightly `-Z
  build-std`), and requires Cygwin `gcc` + LLVM ≥20.1. Fall back to it only if
  the native-binary path translation proves too leaky to finish Route A.
- **Toolchain installs are deferred to the experiment, not the dep pace.** The
  dep-install pace carries only route-independent knowns; `gcc`/`rust`/LLVM are
  Route-C-specific and must not be installed until/unless Route A is abandoned.
- **Scope is the runtime-free tier.** Container tiers (service/crucible) need a
  real container runtime; on Cygwin the only `docker` is Windows Docker Desktop,
  with a separate bind-mount path-translation wall RBTDRX does not cover.
  Per existing discipline, container tests run on the WSL side. This heat
  targets the **fast/validation** tier only.
  *(Superseded 2026-06-03 — see "Progress & scope" above: the crucible tier now
  runs on Cygwin Docker Desktop via the two Windows-docker membranes.)*

## What done looks like

`tt/rbw-ts.TestSuite.fast.sh` runs green over `cygwin@rocket`. A legitimate
alternative outcome is honest: the launch-boundary fix is proven and the
remaining work scoped, with heavier surgery deferred — the experiment is
exploratory and may reveal more than the release timeline wants to absorb.

## Known gaps to close

- **PATH visibility.** `cargo` exists on the host but is not on the
  non-interactive ssh command channel's PATH (rustup wired the Windows user
  PATH; the sshd-spawned shell that runs tabtargets does not see it). Make
  `.cargo/bin` resolvable to that shell — most robustly via the Windows *system*
  PATH or `~/.ssh/environment`.
- **The launch-boundary code change** in `rbtdri`/`rbtdrf` (above).
- **shellcheck path-argument boundary (deferred — off the fast-tier critical
  path).** `shellcheck` has no native Cygwin package (Haskell/GHC is
  incompatible with Cygwin's path layout); the install is the official Windows
  `shellcheck.exe` in `/usr/local/bin` (admin-placed — the unprivileged account
  cannot write there). It resolves and runs, but as a Windows-native tool it
  cannot open Cygwin `/`-style path arguments (`openBinaryFile: does not
  exist`) — it needs Windows-form paths (`cygpath -w`) or stdin. This is
  RBTDRX-family path translation but a **distinct site** from the `.sh`-launch
  boundary above: a Windows *tool* receiving Cygwin path *arguments*, not a
  `.sh` being `CreateProcess`'d. It surfaces only when the harness drives
  shellcheck in `rbw-tr` QualifyRelease — never in the fast/validation tier this
  heat targets. Close it if/when QualifyRelease is brought to Cygwin.

# jjezs_pace ₢BVAAE

## Character
Mechanical cross-platform validation; the one judgment is reading the conntrack verdict.

Validate this heat's Windows-docker changes off-Windows: run the suites on cerebro (native Linux) and rocket's WSL side. The path normalizers are BURD_OSTYPE-gated, so expect identity behavior there — confirm it, no regression. Capture the conntrack_spoofed_ack verdict on each platform; that cross-platform read is the decisive input for the conntrack-adjudication pace — siege and blockade both breach it on Cygwin Docker Desktop, so the open question is whether native Linux/WSL breaches it too.

## Done
siege + blockade green (or differences characterized) on cerebro and WSL, with the conntrack_spoofed_ack verdict recorded per platform.

content.text:
Local check confirms those durable records are on **cerebro**, not this station — so my read of the conntrack case is currently an inference from the commit's aggregate, not a per-case verification. I'll flag that below.

---

## Mount: ₢BVAAE — requal-cerebro-and-wsl

**Pace goal:** Validate this heat's Windows-docker changes *off*-Windows — run the crucible suites on cerebro (native Linux) and rocket's WSL — confirm the `BURD_OSTYPE`-gated path normalizers behave as identity (no regression), and **record the `conntrack_spoofed_ack` verdict per platform**. That cross-platform read is the decisive input for the conntrack-adjudication pace: siege + blockade both *breach* it on uncontrolled Cygwin Docker Desktop, and the open question is whether native Linux / WSL breach it too.

**Heat:** ₣BV (rbk-10-mvp-uncontrolled-cygwin-test), racing, 3/8. The crucible tier now runs on uncontrolled Cygwin DD via two membranes (compose-CLI path normalizer + nested bind-mountpoint pre-creation); the one live finding is the conntrack breach, adjudication pending.

## Opine — yes, the cerebro skirmish substitutes, but only for the cerebro half

I verified the substitution in the suite registry (`rbtdrc_crucible.rs`), so this isn't hand-waving:

- **skirmish** ⊇ `{… TADMOR, MORIAH …}` (lines 2509–2523)
- **siege** = `{kludge-tadmor, TADMOR}`; **blockade** = `{MORIAH}`
- TADMOR and MORIAH both run the shared `RBTDRC_CASES_SECURITY`, and `rbtdrc_sortie_conntrack_spoofed_ack` is in it (line 2639).

So skirmish exercises the *exact* crucible fixtures and the *exact* conntrack sortie that siege + blockade do. On the dimension ₢BVAAE exists to capture, skirmish is a strict superset. The build path differs (siege builds tadmor via local kludge; skirmish via onboarding's kludge+cloud-ordain) — but `conntrack_spoofed_ack` tests runtime network posture inside the charged bottle, which is independent of how the image was built. For *this* verdict, the substitution is clean, not approximate.

Commit `1afc3fcf5` (₢BXAAL) reports the cerebro skirmish as 252 passed / 0 failed / 1 skipped, and names the skip as `rbtdrf_rs_rbrs` (not a security case). So conntrack passed green on cerebro — **native Linux does not breach.** That is exactly the high-value datum the conntrack-adjudication pace wanted: it tips the open question away from "latent gap in the security model" and toward "Cygwin DD platform divergence" (its `nf_conntrack` loose-tracking behavior).

**Where I'd hold the line, honestly:**

1. **WSL is still open.** ₢BVAAE's Done says cerebro **and** WSL, verdict recorded *per platform*. Cerebro-skirmish closes the cerebro half completely; it touches nothing on rocket's WSL. WSL2 is a real Linux kernel and would *likely* match cerebro — but "likely" is the precise thing the adjudication asked us to stop guessing about. Accepting cerebro-skirmish collapses ₢BVAAE to a single remaining task (the WSL run), or it's a deliberate operator scope-call to narrow the pace to cerebro-only. That narrowing is yours to make, not mine.

2. **One inference I haven't verified.** I'm reading "conntrack passed on cerebro" out of the aggregate `0 failed`, not out of the per-case line. The per-case record is in `hist-rbw-ts-skirmish-20260603-132620-1043297-236.txt` — which lives on cerebro, not reachable from here.

Note also: "skirmish *here*" — ₢BVAAE never asked for a run on this darwin station; it asked for cerebro + WSL. So I read "in place of skirmish here" as "instead of me doing a dedicated siege+blockade requal," with the cerebro skirmish standing in for the cerebro leg. Tell me if you meant something narrower.

## What I'd propose

- **Accept** the cerebro skirmish as satisfying the cerebro leg of ₢BVAAE and record `conntrack_spoofed_ack = green (native Linux)` as the per-platform verdict.
- Before recording, optionally `ssh cerebro` and grep that skirmish log for the conntrack case, to turn my inference into a verified per-case PASS.
- Then the only open work in ₢BVAAE is the **WSL leg** — unless you want to make the scope-call to drop it.

Want me to ssh cerebro and confirm the per-case verdict first, or are you content to accept it on the aggregate and move straight to deciding the WSL leg?

content.text:
Good — your management of the cerebro run settles the cerebro leg. Now the curveball: a feature branch + macOS siege→blockade here. Let me think about whether I have real questions before I branch.

I don't have blocking questions, but I do have findings to surface and one thing worth checking myself rather than asking you:

- **siege is fully local** (kludge-tadmor builds sentry+bottle locally, then TADMOR charges/runs/quenches — no GCP/depot). So it's the clean first move, which matches your ordering.
- **blockade is not** — MORIAH is conjure-mode and auto-summons its hallmarks from the depot's GAR. That needs valid retriever credentials *on this station* plus the moriah conjure hallmarks already ordained in the depot. Per the paddock, cross-host credential staleness (`Invalid JWT Signature`) is a known trap. I won't pre-litigate that — if blockade hits it, I'll surface the mechanism + the one repair (re-credential: Payor OAuth reinstall → `dogfight`) and let you call it.
- **The real unknown is whether a container crucible charges on macOS at all.** This heat proved Cygwin DD and cerebro/WSL; macOS/Docker-Desktop-for-Mac is a *new* platform — the sentry's iptables/dnsmasq enclave and bind-mount semantics run inside Desktop's LinuxKit VM, so the first charge is genuinely exploratory. Path normalizers are Cygwin-gated, so they're identity on darwin (like Linux) — no regression risk there.

The thing I'll check rather than ask: whether a docker runtime is even up here.

Branch name: I'll use the active pace coronet ₢BVAAE (ASCII `BVAAE`) + today's datestamp → `bv-macos-crucible-20260603-BVAAE`. One note — ₢BVAAE's silks are "requal-cerebro-and-wsl," which doesn't mention macOS; if you want, I can relabel it or slate a dedicated macOS pace later, but I won't block on that now.

Let me create the branch and probe the runtime.

aiTitle: Review BV mount and git commit history

content.text:
siege passed **clean and complete on macOS** — and the headline datum landed:

**siege (macOS / Docker Desktop) — 60/60, 0 failed, 0 skipped**
- `rbtdro_kludge_tadmor_standalone` (local build of both vessels) — PASSED
- TADMOR crucible: charged → 59 cases → quenched, all PASSED, crucible-inactive confirmed after quench
- **`rbtdrc_sortie_conntrack_spoofed_ack` — PASSED** (line 64). No breach on macOS.

### What this does to the conntrack adjudication

Three platform reads now:

| Platform | conntrack_spoofed_ack |
|---|---|
| cerebro (native Linux) | PASS |
| **macOS Docker Desktop** (LinuxKit VM) | **PASS** |
| Cygwin / Windows Docker Desktop | BREACH |

Both non-Windows platforms pass; only the Windows DD path breaches. That tips the open question hard toward **"Windows-Docker-Desktop-specific divergence," not a latent gap in the security model** — which is the answer the adjudication pace was hunting.

One honesty caveat so I don't overclaim: macOS DD and Windows DD use *different* host-networking stacks (different VM/proxy layers), so a macOS pass doesn't by itself *prove* the Windows breach is benign — it's a third consistent data point, not a controlled isolation. The cleanest isolating comparison is still the WSL leg (same physical rocket box as the Cygwin breach, different docker backend). And separately: **macOS is now demonstrably a viable crucible platform** for this heat — a new capability, likely memo-worthy alongside the Cygwin findings.

### Proceeding to blockade

Per your directive (siege → blockade), launching blockade now. Reminder of the risk: MORIAH is conjure-mode and summons its hallmarks from the depot's GAR, so this needs valid retriever credentials on *this* station. If it hits the stale-cred `Invalid JWT Signature` wall, I'll stop and surface it with the one repair rather than improvising.

aiTitle: Review BV mount and git commit history

content.text:
Cleanup confirmed clean: no moriah containers, no moriah network. The failed charge auto-quenched before bringing anything up (it died at the credential step, upstream of container start). The one stray `canrbhl-srjcl-bottle` is unrelated 2-week-old cruft, not from today.

## blockade failed at the credential wall — not a macOS problem

The charge never reached any macOS-specific mechanism. It died trying to mint a **Retriever** SA token for the auto-summon:

```
WARNING: Sentry vouch artifact missing locally: …/rbi_hm/c260603133211-r260603133214/vouch:…   ← must summon from GAR
Token mint: attempt 1 … (Invalid JWT Signature.) … sleep 2s
… (10 attempts) …
WARNING: Token mint: SA propagation race timeout after 104s (10 attempts)
ERROR: [rbob_charge] Failed to get OAuth token for auto-summon
```

This is the **exact stale-shared-depot-credential signature the paddock warns about** — persistent `Invalid JWT Signature` past the full retry budget. The paddock is explicit that this persistent form is *not* a real propagation race (despite the charge's optimistic label); it's a Retriever SA obsoleted by another host re-mantling the shared Governor (cerebro or cygwin@rocket, this heat).

**Why your OAuth reset didn't clear it:** you reset the *Payor* OAuth — the human credential. But the moriah auto-summon authenticates with the *Retriever SA* (the RBRA key file on this station), which is a different credential and is still stale. Payor reset is necessary but not sufficient. A plain retry won't help either — the SA itself is invalid, not propagating.

## The repair (one), and a consequence you must own

Per the paddock's own recipe: run **`dogfight`** (`tt/rbw-ts.TestSuite.dogfight.sh`). Its `canonical-invest` re-mantles the Governor (now possible — your Payor OAuth is fresh) and re-invests Retriever + Director, writing fresh keys this station owns. Then re-run blockade.

- **Precondition:** dogfight assumes a canonical depot already levied on this station's regime (canonical prefixes installed + `rbw-dL`). If that's not set up here, dogfight will stall at the same investiture step.
- **Cross-host blast radius — your call, not mine:** re-mantling the *shared* Governor from this station will, by the very same mechanism, obsolete the Retriever/Director SAs on **cerebro and cygwin@rocket**. It's a see-saw — fixing macOS re-breaks the others until they re-invest. Since you're stewarding the multi-host credential state, this is squarely your decision.

## Where the macOS picture stands

- **siege (local kludge build)** — fully green, 60/60, `conntrack_spoofed_ack` PASS. macOS is a viable crucible platform for the tether path.
- **blockade (conjure/GAR summon)** — blocked *upstream* of any macOS mechanism, at the credential step. We have **not** yet exercised the macOS airgap charge, so the macOS airgap question remains genuinely open — but the block is environmental, not a platform finding.

Do you want me to run `dogfight` here (accepting the cerebro/cygwin re-break), or defer blockade-on-macOS and bank the siege result for now?

content.text:
Now I have the full picture. `RBTDRK_FIXTURE_CANONICAL_INVEST` is exactly the unit you mean — it's the first fixture in the dogfight suite, and it runs three cases:

1. `rbtdrk_governor_mantle` — re-mantles the Governor (needs the Payor OAuth you just refreshed)
2. `rbtdrk_retriever_invest` — re-invests the Retriever SA (fresh key)
3. `rbtdrk_director_invest` — re-invests the Director SA (fresh key)

The comment confirms it's designed to be composed exactly this way: each case self-probes its precondition against a standing operator-levied depot. skirmish and dogfight both reuse it verbatim. Let me confirm there's no other coupling (tests asserting blockade's shape) before I edit.

content.text:
Only one reference to `blockade` exists — the suite definition itself. No tests or docs hardcode its shape, so the change is clean and local. 

## Assessment: yes, this works

`RBTDRK_FIXTURE_CANONICAL_INVEST` is purpose-built to be composed this way — it's tested machinery (skirmish and dogfight both reuse it verbatim), it self-probes its precondition (a standing operator-levied depot, which this station has — the summon found the canonical GAR target `cancbhm-…`), and its three cases fix exactly the stale-SA wall that blocked the charge. After fresh credentials, MORIAH auto-summons its already-ordained conjure hallmark (`c260603133211-r260603133214`, from the recent ordain-airgap work) and charges. So blockade becomes **self-credentialing**.

Two honest boundaries on what this buys (so the design is legible, not oversold):
- It makes blockade self-*credentialing*, not self-*contained* like siege. It still depends on cloud state: the canonical depot levied **and** the moriah conjure hallmark present in GAR. canonical-invest fixes credentials, not a missing hallmark.
- The **cross-host see-saw is now baked into blockade**: every run re-mantles the *shared* Governor, obsoleting cerebro/cygwin SAs until they re-invest. That's the same consequence as the dogfight path — but it's now blockade's standing behavior, joining the skirmish/dogfight self-credentialing family. I'll document that in the suite comment so it's not a surprise later.

Proceeding. Editing the suite:

content.text:
Now I understand the full picture — and there's both a win and a genuine fork for you.

## The credential heal works — that's the important win

```
PASSED:  rbtdrk_governor_mantle      ← Payor OAuth reset took; Governor re-mantled
PASSED:  rbtdrk_retriever_invest     ← Retriever SA re-invested fresh
FAILED:  rbtdrk_director_invest
```

The two cases blockade actually depends on **passed**. The original stale-Retriever wall — the thing that blocked the moriah summon — is gone. The retriever is the credential the conjure-summon uses, and it's now freshly invested on this station.

## Why director_invest failed — and why it's extraneous here

```
rbgg_invest_director: Service account created: director-canest-dir@…  ✓
rbgg_invest_director: Adding Cloud Build Editor role (project scope)
ERROR: Grant Cloud Build Editor (set policy) (HTTP 400):
       This resource is under organization policy controls which are
       incompatible with the provided policy.
```

The SA and key got created; the failure is purely on the **Cloud Build Editor IAM grant**, rejected by an **organization policy**. That's a Pale-boundary refusal (GCP org admin), not something this repo controls.

Here's the load-bearing point: **blockade doesn't need the director at all.** The director's role is to *ordain* (Cloud Build → push to GAR). blockade only *summons* an already-ordained hallmark, which uses the **retriever**. I imported the whole `canonical-invest` fixture wholesale, but its third case invests a credential blockade never uses — and that's the only case that failed. The clean fix is to trim blockade's prep to exactly what it uses: `governor_mantle` + `retriever_invest`.

## Two things worth your eyes before I pick a direction

1. **The org-policy wall is probably broader than blockade.** skirmish and dogfight *also* re-invest the director through `canonical-invest`, so they'd hit this same HTTP 400 on this depot. Yet the recent `ordain-airgap` / conjure commits succeeded with a director — so this smells like a **recent org-policy change** on `cancbhm-d-canest3bhm100001`, not a standing condition. If so, it'll bite the director-using suites next time they re-invest. I can't resolve an org policy from here (Pale), but you'd want to know.

2. Right now the retriever is freshly invested (side effect of this run), so a bare MORIAH charge would likely work *this minute* — but that's fragile; the durable answer is the trim.

## The fork — your call

- **(a) Trim & proceed:** add a minimal `governor_mantle + retriever_invest` prep fixture, point blockade at it, and run. Gets blockade green on macOS now (finally exercising the airgap charge + the `conntrack_spoofed_ack` verdict), and sidesteps the org-policy wall cleanly because blockade legitimately doesn't need the director.
- **(b) Pause and dig:** treat the director org-policy 400 as the headline finding and investigate it first (it likely affects skirmish/dogfight), before touching blockade further.

I lean (a) — it's the correct load-bearing shape for blockade regardless, and it doesn't bury (1): I'd still flag the org-policy wall as a separate finding for you to chase. But you flagged blockade as unfamiliar ground, and the org-policy change could be the more urgent signal — so I'd rather you steer. Which way?

content.text:
Pulled the exact sequence. You're right, and my framing was inverted — let me own that plainly and show why.

## You're right: blockade is malformed

The onboarding-sequence cases that produce **moriah's** images are:

```
rbtdro_onboarding_inscribe_reliquary      ← yokes reliquary stamp into all ordain-path vessels (precondition)
rbtdro_onboarding_ordain_conjure_sentry   ← conjure rbev-sentry-deb-tether → RBRN_SENTRY_HALLMARK → moriah
rbtdro_onboarding_ordain_airgap_chain     ← enshrine rust base→forge, conjure forge, conjure airgap bottle → RBRN_BOTTLE_HALLMARK → moriah
```

Every one of those is a **Director + Cloud Build** operation (ordain / enshrine / conjure). moriah's sentry comes from `ordain_conjure_sentry`; its bottle comes from the `ordain_airgap_chain`. So moriah's two vessels are *produced* by the director — exactly as you said.

My error: I scoped blockade as "charge an already-ordained moriah" and concluded the director was extraneous. But that scoping **is** the malformation. Current blockade — even with my canonical-invest prepend — is charge-only; it presupposes that someone, somewhere, already ran those ordains (the recent `ordain-conjure` / `ordain-airgap` commits, on whatever station did them). It free-rides on external state. That's not standalone; it's a suite that silently depends on a hallmark it doesn't make.

The siege parallel makes it stark:

| | produces images | charges + ifrit |
|---|---|---|
| **siege** | `kludge-tadmor` (local docker build, both vessels) | `TADMOR` |
| **blockade (correct)** | *ordain moriah* (conjure sentry + airgap chain) — **director** | `MORIAH` |
| **blockade (current)** | — *(missing)* | `MORIAH` |

siege is standalone because it builds its own images. blockade dropped the entire "produce the images" half. The conjure-mode analog of "kludge builds locally" is "director ordains via Cloud Build" — so a standalone blockade is *inherently* director-dependent. You can't have airgap-mode without the ordain.

## This flips everything I recommended

- **The director is load-bearing.** My "trim it" was wrong — trimming would permanently cement the free-riding.
- **The org-policy 400 is now a hard blocker, not something to route around.** A standalone blockade must grant the director Cloud Build access to ordain moriah; that grant is exactly what the org policy is rejecting. Option (b) — confront the org-policy wall — is the necessary path.
- And it likely isn't blockade-specific: skirmish and dogfight re-invest the same director through `canonical-invest`, so this `cancbhm` depot would reject *their* director grant too. Since the recent ordain commits succeeded, this smells like a **recent org-policy change** on the project (or those ordains ran against a different depot/org). That's plausibly a Pale-boundary item — GCP org admin, which I can't fix from inside the repo.

## Where that leaves us

The correct standalone blockade is essentially **"the moriah slice of skirmish"**:

```
canonical-invest  (governor + retriever + director)
  → ordain-moriah (inscribe_reliquary + ordain_conjure_sentry + ordain_airgap_chain)   ← new fixture, cherry-picking existing cases
  → MORIAH        (charge + ifrit + quench)
```

My commit `3e7587c8` got the credential half partly right (it correctly keeps the director — we *do* need it) but is still incomplete: no ordain predecessor. And none of the ordain steps can run until the Cloud Build Editor grant succeeds.

So I think the real first question is the org-policy 400, not the fixture wiring — because the fixture is moot until the director can build. Two ways to go:

- **Dig the org policy first:** I can pull the project's effective org policies (`gcloud resource-manager org-policies list` / the constraint behind a setIamPolicy 400) to characterize the wall precisely, and we figure out whether it's a recent change and whether it's yours to lift. This also tells us if skirmish/dogfight are about to break on `cancbhm`.
- **Design the standalone fixture now**, so it's ready the moment the grant works — but it'd sit un-runnable until then.

My read: chase the org policy first. Want me to go pull the effective policies on `cancbhm-d-canest3bhm100001`?

content: Goal: prove the macOS crucible on branch bv-macos-crucible-20260603-BVAAE. siege passed 60/60 (conntrack PASS); blockade is malformed because it never ordains moriah's images, which needs the Director, now blocked by an org-policy 400. Next: pull effective org policies on cancbhm-d-canest3bhm100001. (disable recaps in /config)

The payor is the manor owner — the identity most likely to hold `orgpolicy.policies.list`. Let me mint a payor access token from the refresh token and run the queries with it. I'll keep secrets out of the output.

input.command:
set +x
base=/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_beta_recipemuster/../station-files/secrets
CS="$base/client_secrets/client_secret_727189059962-ddv3midird5bvukmca6na61d2r8d4i4j.apps.googleusercontent.com.json"
command -v jq >/dev/null || { echo "jq missing"; exit 2; }
CID=$(jq -r '(.installed//.web).client_id' "$CS")
# refresh token + client secret from freshest payor rbro.env
. "$base/payor/rbro.env"
RESP=$(curl -s -X POST https://oauth2.googleapis.com/token \
  -d client_id="$CID" \
  -d client_secret="$RBRO_CLIENT_SECRET" \
  -d refresh_token="$RBRO_REFRESH_TOKEN" \
  -d grant_type=refresh_token)
TOK=$(printf '%s' "$RESP" | jq -r '.access_token // empty')
if [ -z "$TOK" ]; then
  echo "TOKEN MINT FAILED:"; printf '%s' "$RESP" | jq -r '{error,error_description,scope}' 2>&1
  exit 1
fi
echo "payor token minted ok (len ${#TOK})"
# what scopes / identity does this token carry?
echo "=== tokeninfo (scopes + email) ==="
curl -s "https://oauth2.googleapis.com/tokeninfo?access_token=$TOK" | jq -r '{email,scope,expires_in}' 2>&1
export CLOUDSDK_AUTH_ACCESS_TOKEN="$TOK"
P=cancbhm-d-canest3bhm100001
echo "=== project describe ==="
gcloud projects describe "$P" --format='value(projectId,parent.type,parent.id)' 2>&1
echo "=== org-policies on project ==="

The v2 `org-policies` API isn't enabled on the OAuth client's quota project, but `cloudresourcemanager` clearly is (the `projects describe` succeeded), so I'll use the **v1 `resource-manager org-policies`** path, which rides that API. The error signature ("incompatible with the provided policy" on a setIamPolicy) is the classic fingerprint of Domain Restricted Sharing — let me check that constraint's effective policy directly, plus list everything set.

content.text:
The wall is identified: **`constraints/iam.allowedPolicyMemberDomains`** — Domain Restricted Sharing — set at the org and allowing only customer **`C02ystilf`** (scaleinvariant.org's Cloud Identity customer). That's the exact constraint whose signature is "incompatible with the provided policy."

The classic mechanism: the director grant does a read-modify-write `setIamPolicy`, and if the project's *existing* policy already contains a member outside `C02ystilf`, the whole resubmitted policy is rejected — even when adding an allowed member. A prime suspect is `bhyslop@gmail.com` (the stale login, a personal account). Let me read the project's IAM policy and find any member outside scaleinvariant.org.

input.command:
set +x
base=/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_beta_recipemuster/../station-files/secrets
CS="$base/client_secrets/client_secret_727189059962-ddv3midird5bvukmca6na61d2r8d4i4j.apps.googleusercontent.com.json"
CID=$(jq -r '(.installed//.web).client_id' "$CS")
. "$base/payor/rbro.env"
TOK=$(curl -s -X POST https://oauth2.googleapis.com/token -d client_id="$CID" -d client_secret="$RBRO_CLIENT_SECRET" -d refresh_token="$RBRO_REFRESH_TOKEN" -d grant_type=refresh_token | jq -r '.access_token // empty')
[ -z "$TOK" ] && { echo "token mint failed"; exit 1; }
export CLOUDSDK_AUTH_ACCESS_TOKEN="$TOK"
P=cancbhm-d-canest3bhm100001
echo "=== all distinct members in project IAM policy ==="
gcloud projects get-iam-policy "$P" --format='json' 2>/dev/null | jq -r '[.bindings[].members[]] | unique | .[]'
echo "=== >>> members NOT @scaleinvariant.org and NOT in-project SAs (DRS violators) <<< ==="
gcloud projects get-iam-policy "$P" --format='json' 2>/dev/null | jq -r --arg P "$P" '[.bindings[].members[]] | unique | .[] | select((endswith("@scaleinvariant.org")|not) and (endswith("@" + $P + ".iam.gserviceaccount.com")|not))'

aiTitle: Review BV mount and git commit history

content.text: This is the real story, and it's **not** a hostile org policy — it's accumulated rot. Let me get exact counts to nail it.

input.command:
set +x
base=/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_beta_recipemuster/../station-files/secrets
CS="$base/client_secrets/client_secret_727189059962-ddv3midird5bvukmca6na61d2r8d4i4j.apps.googleusercontent.com.json"
CID=$(jq -r '(.installed//.web).client_id' "$CS")
. "$base/payor/rbro.env"
TOK=$(curl -s -X POST https://oauth2.googleapis.com/token -d client_id="$CID" -d client_secret="$RBRO_CLIENT_SECRET" -d refresh_token="$RBRO_REFRESH_TOKEN" -d grant_type=refresh_token | jq -r '.access_token // empty')
[ -z "$TOK" ] && { echo "token mint failed"; exit 1; }
export CLOUDSDK_AUTH_ACCESS_TOKEN="$TOK"
P=cancbhm-d-canest3bhm100001
POL=$(gcloud projects get-iam-policy "$P" --format='json' 2>/dev/null)
echo "=== member counts by class ==="
printf '%s' "$POL" | jq -r '
  [.bindings[].members[]] as $m
  | {
      total: ($m|length),
      distinct: ($m|unique|length),
      deleted_tombstones: ($m|map(select(startswith("deleted:")))|length),
      deleted_distinct: ($m|map(select(startswith("deleted:")))|unique|length),
      live_human: ($m|map(select(startswith("user:")))|unique),
      live_google_agents: ($m|map(select((startswith("serviceAccount:")) and ((contains("gserviceaccount.com")) and ((contains(".iam.gserviceaccount.com")|not) or contains("gcp-sa-") or contains("@container-analysis") ))))|unique)
    }'
echo "=== deleted tombstones grouped by SA basename ==="
printf '%s' "$POL" | jq -r '[.bindings[].members[]|select(startswith("deleted:"))]|map(sub("\\?uid=.*";"")|sub("deleted:serviceAccount:";""))|group_by(.)|map({sa:.[0],count:length})|sort_by(-.count)[]|"\(.count)\t\(.sa)"'
echo "=== which bindings (roles) still reference deleted members ==="
printf '%s' "$POL" | jq -r '.bindings[]|select(any(.members[];startswith("deleted:")))|{role:.role,deleted_in_binding:([.members[]|select(startswith("deleted:"))]|length)}|"\(.deleted_in_binding)\t\(.role)"' | sort -rn

aiTitle: Review BV mount and git commit history

content.text:
The investigation paid off — and it flips the framing entirely. **The org policy is legitimate; the rot is ours.**

## What's actually on the project

`cancbhm-d-canest3bhm100001` is under org `247899326218`, and its IAM policy has:

| | count |
|---|---|
| total member entries | **157** |
| `deleted:serviceAccount:…?uid=` tombstones | **145** (76 distinct) |
| live humans | 1 — `user:bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org` ✓ allowed |
| live Google service agents | 5 (DRS-exempt) |

The tombstones, by SA: **69 dead `director-canest-dir`**, **46 dead `retriever-canest-ret`**, **30 dead datestamped `governor-*`**. And the role bindings carrying them:

```
30  roles/owner                              ← dead governors
23  roles/cloudbuild.builds.editor           ← dead directors  ← the exact role the grant adds
23  roles/cloudbuild.workerPoolUser
23  roles/artifactregistry.reader
23  roles/viewer
23  roles/containeranalysis.occurrences.viewer
```

## The mechanism

The org constraint is **`iam.allowedPolicyMemberDomains`** (Domain Restricted Sharing), set at the org, allowing only customer `C02ystilf` (scaleinvariant.org). That's a normal, protective policy — not a hostile or recent change. The only live human is in-domain; the Google agents are exempt. **The single source of DRS-incompatible content in the policy is the 145 `deleted:` tombstones.**

`rbgg_invest_director`'s grant of `cloudbuild.builds.editor` is a project-scope read-modify-write `setIamPolicy`. It reads the whole policy — all 145 tombstones included — adds the new director, and writes it back. DRS validates every member in the submitted policy and can't resolve a *deleted* principal against the allowed customer, so it rejects the whole thing: HTTP 400, "incompatible with the provided policy." The `cloudbuild.builds.editor` binding already carries 23 dead directors, so the grant is poisoned before it even adds the 24th.

**This is a lifecycle leak in our own tooling:** divest/replace deletes the old SA but never removes its role bindings first, so every canonical-invest cycle drops a fresh tombstone. The director was last cleanly invested ~May 16 (`director/rbra.env.bak-20260516-stale100009`); the churn since then crossed the line, and blockade is just the first thing in a while to force a director re-invest and trip it.

## Confidence, honestly

- **High:** DRS is the constraint; 145 tombstones are the incompatible content; the director grant resubmits them.
- **Medium / inferred:** that DRS rejects specifically because *deleted* principals can't be validated — that's the standard mechanism, and the cleanup below would confirm it.
- **Open:** why `governor_mantle` and `retriever_invest` *passed* today while `director_invest` failed, when all three churn SAs. Likely they grant via incremental `add-iam-policy-binding` (delta-validated) or at a narrower resource, while the director does a full project-scope `setIamPolicy`. I haven't read the `rbgg` grant code per-role to confirm — that's the next thread.

## Remediation — two layers, and a mutation gate

1. **One-time cleanup (mutates project IAM):** strip the 145 `deleted:` members from the policy (getIamPolicy → drop `deleted:*` → setIamPolicy). The cleaned policy contains only in-domain + exempt members, so it passes DRS, and the director grant then succeeds. Only dead principals are touched — no live identity is affected — but it *is* a project-IAM write, so I won't do it without your say-so.
2. **Durable fix (the real bug):** make divest remove role bindings *before* deleting the SA (the director/retriever/governor divest paths in `rbgi`/`rbgg`), so tombstones stop re-accumulating. Without this, cleanup is temporary.

And independently of all that, blockade still needs its ordain predecessor — but this tombstone wall is upstream of everything: it'll keep breaking skirmish/dogfight's director re-invest too, silently bloating the policy each run.

How do you want to play it? I'd suggest: (a) you authorize the one-time tombstone purge so the director can be invested again, then (b) I read the `rbgg`/`rbgi` divest paths to pin the leak and propose the durable fix. But the purge is your call — want me to run it (I'll show you the exact cleaned-vs-current diff before writing)?

Next: get your OK to purge those tombstones (I'll show the diff first). (disable recaps in /config)

content.text:
Ha — yes. You reset reading me; I reset between sessions. Two forgetful collaborators trying to hand off cleanly. Let me make it landable.

**The whole thing, short:**

1. You were right: blockade is malformed — it never *builds* moriah's images, it borrows ones built elsewhere.
2. But tonight's real wall is upstream of that: our own tooling leaves a dead service-account "permission ghost" behind every time it recreates one. The project has piled up **145** of them.
3. A normal org safety rule now refuses to re-grant the director, because that whole pile of ghosts rides along in the request. Nothing hostile — just our litter.
4. Fix order: sweep the ghosts (one safe write — touches only already-dead accounts) → stop the leak (divest should drop permissions *before* deleting) → *then* give blockade its missing ordain step.

siege is green and banked. The one commit on our branch is a partial step, not wrong.

My honest rec: a project-IAM write is not a thing to start as you're fading. Let me **write this down so it survives both our resets**, and we do the sweep + fix fresh.

198:An S3 bucket (with Object Lock in Compliance mode for retention), an IAM role with a trust policy accepting the envoy's federated identity, and Recipe-Bottle-shipped IAM policy shapes that the AWS consumer attaches to whatever principal consumes delivered artifacts (EC2 role, ECS task role, Bedrock execution role, etc.).
213:it federates to AWS via OIDC Workload Identity Federation, exchanging short-lived GCP tokens for short-lived AWS STS credentials at operation time.
214:Preserves credential locality: Director holds only GCP credentials on the workstation, envoy holds only federation configuration in GCP, and AWS admin credentials never enter Recipe Bottle's custody.
229:Accredit: governor configures the envoy's OIDC federation against an embassy's IAM role trust policy, and records the binding (embassy name, AWS account, region, role ARN, bucket name) in depot state.
230:Recall: governor removes the federation configuration and prunes the binding.
238:The envoy executes the cross-post from within Cloud Build: federates to the embassy's IAM role, streams bytes to S3, applies Object Lock retention.
297:**Operator federation — external-IdP identity for depot participants.**
304:Operator federation is the heavyweight successor tier for installations that cannot accept an operator secret on disk.
307:to trust an external OIDC/SAML identity provider (any compliant issuer — self-hosted Dex/Keycloak, or GCP Identity Platform as an in-house IdP), then grants capabilities to those federated `principal://` subjects: keyless, no Google accounts for participants, revocation central at the depot.
311:Org-only — federation pools are organization-level (Workforce Identity Federation, the human-identity product), so a no-org
313:cannot use this tier; the organization requirement is the line between the free keyfile tier and the federation tier.
316:Distinct from the envoy's egress federation (GCP-to-AWS delivery, the machine-identity Workload product): this is
320:_Reference_: `Memos/memo-20260527-operator-credential-models.md` (the source document for this entry — the committed two-tier plan: keyfile (free, no-org) and Workforce federation (paid, org-required), the curl-native two-leg flow, device-flow / 12-hour session cap, the per-depot mode enum homed in RBRD, and the cult-verb shape);
323:Google docs: Workforce Identity Federation overview (`https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/workforce-identity-federation`) and short-lived token exchange (`https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/workforce-obtaining-short-lived-credentials`).

zrbgg_divest_role() {
  zrbgg_sentinel

  local -r z_role="$1"
  local -r z_identity="$2"
  local -r z_role_rbra_file="$3"

  local z_account_email="${z_role}-${z_identity}@${RBGD_SA_EMAIL_FULL}"

  buc_step "Divesting service account: ${z_account_email}"

  buc_log_args 'Get OAuth token from admin'
  local z_token
  z_token=$(rba_get_governor_token_capture) || buc_die "Failed to get admin token"

  buc_log_args 'Delete via REST API (404-tolerant)'
  rbuh_json "DELETE" "${RBGD_API_SERVICE_ACCOUNTS}/${z_account_email}" "${z_token}" \
                                                 "${ZRBGG_INFIX_DELETE}"
  rbuh_require_ok "Delete service account" "${ZRBGG_INFIX_DELETE}" \
    404 "not found (already deleted)"

  buc_log_args 'Confirm deletion propagated before any same-name recreate'
  # When the DELETE actually removed an SA, wait until the GET path reports it
  # gone — a delete-accepted response precedes propagation, and an immediately
  # following same-name invest would otherwise race a still-visible account.
  # Skipped on 404: the account was already absent, nothing to wait for.
  local z_delete_code
  z_delete_code=$(rbuh_code_capture "${ZRBGG_INFIX_DELETE}") || z_delete_code=""
  if test "${z_delete_code}" != "404"; then
    rbuh_poll_until_gone "Service account ${z_account_email}" \
      "${RBGD_API_SERVICE_ACCOUNTS}/${z_account_email}" "${z_token}" "${ZRBGG_INFIX_DELETE_GONE}"
  fi

  buc_log_args 'Opportunistic role-RBRA cleanup if installed credential matches'
  if test -f "${z_role_rbra_file}"; then
    local z_installed_email
    z_installed_email=$(. "${z_role_rbra_file}" 2>/dev/null && printf '%s' "${RBRA_CLIENT_EMAIL:-}") \
      || z_installed_email=""
    if test "${z_installed_email}" = "${z_account_email}"; then
      rm -f "${z_role_rbra_file}"
      buc_info "Removed installed RBRA at ${z_role_rbra_file} (matched divested email)"
    fi
  fi

  buc_success "Divest operation completed"
}

rbgg_divest_retriever() {
  zrbgg_sentinel

  local z_identity="${BUZ_FOLIO:-}"

  buc_doc_brief "Divest a Retriever service account by identity"
  buc_doc_param "identity" "Identity (required) — composes ${RBCC_account_retriever}-<identity>"
  buc_doc_shown || return 0

  test -n "${z_identity}" || buc_die "Identity required"

  zrbgg_divest_role "${RBCC_account_retriever}" "${z_identity}" "${RBDC_RETRIEVER_RBRA_FILE}"
}

rbgg_divest_director() {
  zrbgg_sentinel

  local z_identity="${BUZ_FOLIO:-}"

  buc_doc_brief "Divest a Director service account by identity"
  buc_doc_param "identity" "Identity (required) — composes ${RBCC_account_director}-<identity>"
  buc_doc_shown || return 0

  test -n "${z_identity}" || buc_die "Identity required"

  zrbgg_divest_role "${RBCC_account_director}" "${z_identity}" "${RBDC_DIRECTOR_RBRA_FILE}"
}

272	  # it was non-load-bearing and is gone.
273	  buc_log_args "${z_label}: granted (setIamPolicy 200)"
274	  return 0

rbgi_add_project_iam_role() {
  zrbgi_sentinel

  local -r z_token="${1:-}"
  local -r z_label="${2:-}"
  local -r z_resource="${3:-}"  # resource_base: Base resource URL
  local -r z_role="${4:-}"
  local -r z_member="${5:-}"
  local -r z_parent_infix="${6:-}"

  test -n "${z_token}" || buc_die "Token required"
  buc_log_args "Using admin token (value not logged)"

  local -r z_resource_path="${z_resource#/}"  # strip leading slash if present
  local -r z_base="${RBGC_API_ROOT_CRM}${RBGC_CRM_V1}/${z_resource_path}"
  local -r z_get_url="${z_base}${RBGC_CRM_GET_IAM_POLICY_SUFFIX}"
  local -r z_set_url="${z_base}${RBGC_CRM_SET_IAM_POLICY_SUFFIX}"

  test -n "${z_resource}" || buc_die "resource required"
  test -n "${z_role}"     || buc_die "role required"
  test -n "${z_member}"   || buc_die "member required"

  buc_log_args "${z_label}: add ${z_member} to ${z_role}"

  # Propagation retry — project-scope tolerance: forward/backward
  # member-visibility (400 patterns). Class C (403 resource-scope cache lag)
  # does not apply at project scope.
  local -ra z_tolerance=(
    "400" "*does not exist*"
    "400" "*is not deleted*"
  )
  local z_prop_delay=${RBGC_PROPAGATION_INITIAL_DELAY_SEC}
  local z_prop_elapsed=0
  local -r z_prop_deadline=${RBGC_PROPAGATION_DEADLINE_SEC}
  local z_prop_attempt=0
  local z_prop_succeeded=0

  while :; do
    z_prop_attempt=$((z_prop_attempt + 1))

    buc_log_args "1) GET policy (v3) [attempt ${z_prop_attempt}]"
    buc_log_args "GET_POLICY_URL_DEBUG z_resource:${z_resource} z_get_url:${z_get_url}"
    local z_get_body="${ZRBGI_PREFIX}${z_parent_infix}_get_body.json"
    local z_get_infix="${z_parent_infix}-get-${z_prop_elapsed}s"
    printf '%s\n' '{"options":{"requestedPolicyVersion":3}}' > "${z_get_body}"
    rbuh_json "POST" "${z_get_url}" "${z_token}" "${z_get_infix}" "${z_get_body}"

    local z_get_code=""
    z_get_code=$(rbuh_code_capture "${z_get_infix}") || buc_die "No HTTP code from getIamPolicy"

    # Check for propagation error on GET
    if zrbgi_propagation_error_predicate "${z_get_infix}" "${z_get_code}" "${z_tolerance[@]}"; then
      buc_log_args "${z_label}: getIamPolicy returned ${z_get_code} (propagation delay)"
      test "${z_prop_elapsed}" -lt "${z_prop_deadline}" \
        || buc_die "${z_label}: propagation timeout after ${z_prop_elapsed}s waiting for member visibility"
      buc_log_args "Retry ${z_prop_attempt} at ${z_prop_elapsed}s (next delay ${z_prop_delay}s)"
      sleep "${z_prop_delay}"
      z_prop_elapsed=$((z_prop_elapsed + z_prop_delay))
      z_prop_delay=$((z_prop_delay * 2))
      test "${z_prop_delay}" -le "${RBGC_PROPAGATION_MAX_DELAY_SEC}" || z_prop_delay=${RBGC_PROPAGATION_MAX_DELAY_SEC}
      continue
    fi

    # Not a propagation error on GET — require success
    rbuh_require_ok "${z_label} (get policy)" "${z_get_infix}"

    buc_log_args 'Extract etag; require non-empty'
    local z_etag=""
    z_etag=$(rbuh_json_field_capture "${z_get_infix}" ".etag") || buc_die "Missing etag"
    test -n "${z_etag}" || buc_die "Empty etag"

    buc_log_args "Using etag ${z_etag}"

    buc_log_args '2) Build new policy JSON in temp (bindings unique; version=3; keep etag)'
    local z_new_policy_json=""
    z_new_policy_json=$(rbgi_jq_add_member_to_role_capture "${z_get_infix}" "${z_role}" "${z_member}" "${z_etag}") \
      || buc_die "Failed to compose policy JSON"

    local z_set_body="${ZRBGI_PREFIX}${z_parent_infix}_set_body.json"
    printf '{"policy":%s}\n' "${z_new_policy_json}" > "${z_set_body}"

    buc_log_args '3) setIamPolicy (fatal on 409 — etag mismatch)'
    local z_set_elapsed=0
    local z_set_infix=""
    local z_set_succeeded=0
    while :; do
      z_set_infix="${z_parent_infix}-set-${z_prop_elapsed}s-${z_set_elapsed}s"
      rbuh_json "POST" "${z_set_url}" "${z_token}" "${z_set_infix}" "${z_set_body}"

      local z_code=""
      z_code=$(rbuh_code_capture "${z_set_infix}") || buc_die "No HTTP code"

      # Check for propagation error on SET — break inner loop to retry outer
      if zrbgi_propagation_error_predicate "${z_set_infix}" "${z_code}" "${z_tolerance[@]}"; then
        buc_log_args "${z_label}: setIamPolicy returned ${z_code} (propagation delay)"
        break
      fi

      case "${z_code}" in
        200)                 z_set_succeeded=1; break ;;
        409)                 buc_die "${z_label}: HTTP 409 ABORTED (etag mismatch — concurrent policy change)" ;;
        429|500|502|503|504) buc_log_args "Transient ${z_code} at ${z_set_elapsed}s; retry" ;;
        *)                   rbuh_require_ok "${z_label} (set policy)" "${z_set_infix}" "" ;;
      esac

      test "${z_set_elapsed}" -lt "${RBGC_MAX_CONSISTENCY_SEC}" || buc_die "${z_label}: timeout setting policy"
      sleep "${RBGC_EVENTUAL_CONSISTENCY_SEC}"
      z_set_elapsed=$((z_set_elapsed + RBGC_EVENTUAL_CONSISTENCY_SEC))
    done

    # If setIamPolicy succeeded, break outer propagation loop
    test "${z_set_succeeded}" != "1" || { z_prop_succeeded=1; break; }

    # setIamPolicy hit propagation error — retry outer loop with fresh getIamPolicy
    test "${z_prop_elapsed}" -lt "${z_prop_deadline}" \
      || buc_die "${z_label}: propagation timeout after ${z_prop_elapsed}s waiting for member visibility"
    buc_log_args "Retry ${z_prop_attempt} at ${z_prop_elapsed}s (next delay ${z_prop_delay}s)"
    sleep "${z_prop_delay}"
    z_prop_elapsed=$((z_prop_elapsed + z_prop_delay))
    z_prop_delay=$((z_prop_delay * 2))
    test "${z_prop_delay}" -le "${RBGC_PROPAGATION_MAX_DELAY_SEC}" || z_prop_delay=${RBGC_PROPAGATION_MAX_DELAY_SEC}
  done

  test "${z_prop_succeeded}" = "1" || buc_die "${z_label}: propagation retry loop exited without success"

  # The setIamPolicy 200 is the authoritative grant confirmation: the policy was
  # written atomically with this binding. A post-set read-back verify loop was
  # removed here. Against a standing depot, repeated divest/invest cycles leave
  # same-email `deleted:serviceAccount:...?uid=` tombstones in the policy (accepted
  # — re-levy is quota-limited), and the email→uid read reconciliation can lag the
  # getIamPolicy read past any bounded poll, producing a false timeout on a grant
  # that already succeeded. The read-back never made the grant effective — that
  # propagates on GCP's clock regardless and cannot be accelerated by polling — so
  # it was non-load-bearing and is gone.
  buc_log_args "${z_label}: granted (setIamPolicy 200)"
  return 0
}

rbgi_add_repo_iam_role() {
  zrbgi_sentinel

content.text: This is the crux, and the code answers it precisely — there's even a comment that already *names* the accumulation as a known, "accepted" tradeoff. Let me read the operator-federation section of the roadmap so I connect the fix correctly.

293	Requires reproducible toolchains — a vessel-author discipline, not a pipeline control.
294	_Trigger_: vessel ecosystem maturity and external distribution.
295	_Reference_: RBSCB-CloudBuildPosture.adoc (Deferred Items: Reproducibility).
296	
297	**Operator federation — external-IdP identity for depot participants.**
298	Recipe Bottle grants
299	{rbtgi_depot}
300	capabilities to identities the depot's IAM recognizes.
301	The shipping model is the keyfile tier: each operator role holds a service-account key file (the
302	{rbtgi_rbra_file})
303	on disk, possession of the key is the identity, and no GCP organization is required.
304	Operator federation is the heavyweight successor tier for installations that cannot accept an operator secret on disk.
305	The depot owner configures the
306	{rbtgi_depot}
307	to trust an external OIDC/SAML identity provider (any compliant issuer — self-hosted Dex/Keycloak, or GCP Identity Platform as an in-house IdP), then grants capabilities to those federated `principal://` subjects: keyless, no Google accounts for participants, revocation central at the depot.
308	The
309	{rbtgi_depot}
310	remains the home of the participant roster (the role-to-identity mapping); the IdP owns only identity proofing.
311	Org-only — federation pools are organization-level (Workforce Identity Federation, the human-identity product), so a no-org
312	{rbtr_payor}
313	cannot use this tier; the organization requirement is the line between the free keyfile tier and the federation tier.
314	A qualifying organization is free via Cloud Identity once its owner verifies control of a DNS domain, so the true prerequisite is that the operator controls a domain — not that they pay Google.
315	The Google-side credential path is curl-native — an STS token exchange (RFC 8693), no gcloud and no added stack dependency; only the IdP-auth leg (device flow where the issuer supports it) varies by issuer.
316	Distinct from the envoy's egress federation (GCP-to-AWS delivery, the machine-identity Workload product): this is
317	{at_operator}
318	*access* identity arriving the other way.
319	_Trigger_: first concrete need from an organization that cannot accept on-disk operator keyfiles, or when the keyfile tier's secret-distribution and rotation burden materially blocks adoption rather than being theoretically frictionful.
320	_Reference_: `Memos/memo-20260527-operator-credential-models.md` (the source document for this entry — the committed two-tier plan: keyfile (free, no-org) and Workforce federation (paid, org-required), the curl-native two-leg flow, device-flow / 12-hour session cap, the per-depot mode enum homed in RBRD, and the cult-verb shape);
321	`Memos/memo-20260522-org-affiliated-credential-reorientation.md` (R1/R2/R3 credential model and three-tier ladder, plus R4/R5 test-rig and gauntlet resolutions);
322	`Memos/memo-20260427-google-native-human-auth.md` (impersonation mechanism detail — its human-auth *framing* is superseded by R2);
323	Google docs: Workforce Identity Federation overview (`https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/workforce-identity-federation`) and short-lived token exchange (`https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/workforce-obtaining-short-lived-credentials`).

Requires reproducible toolchains — a vessel-author discipline, not a pipeline control.
_Trigger_: vessel ecosystem maturity and external distribution.
_Reference_: RBSCB-CloudBuildPosture.adoc (Deferred Items: Reproducibility).

**Operator federation — external-IdP identity for depot participants.**
Recipe Bottle grants
{rbtgi_depot}
capabilities to identities the depot's IAM recognizes.
The shipping model is the keyfile tier: each operator role holds a service-account key file (the
{rbtgi_rbra_file})
on disk, possession of the key is the identity, and no GCP organization is required.
Operator federation is the heavyweight successor tier for installations that cannot accept an operator secret on disk.
The depot owner configures the
{rbtgi_depot}
to trust an external OIDC/SAML identity provider (any compliant issuer — self-hosted Dex/Keycloak, or GCP Identity Platform as an in-house IdP), then grants capabilities to those federated `principal://` subjects: keyless, no Google accounts for participants, revocation central at the depot.
The
{rbtgi_depot}
remains the home of the participant roster (the role-to-identity mapping); the IdP owns only identity proofing.
Org-only — federation pools are organization-level (Workforce Identity Federation, the human-identity product), so a no-org
{rbtr_payor}
cannot use this tier; the organization requirement is the line between the free keyfile tier and the federation tier.
A qualifying organization is free via Cloud Identity once its owner verifies control of a DNS domain, so the true prerequisite is that the operator controls a domain — not that they pay Google.
The Google-side credential path is curl-native — an STS token exchange (RFC 8693), no gcloud and no added stack dependency; only the IdP-auth leg (device flow where the issuer supports it) varies by issuer.
Distinct from the envoy's egress federation (GCP-to-AWS delivery, the machine-identity Workload product): this is
{at_operator}
*access* identity arriving the other way.
_Trigger_: first concrete need from an organization that cannot accept on-disk operator keyfiles, or when the keyfile tier's secret-distribution and rotation burden materially blocks adoption rather than being theoretically frictionful.
_Reference_: `Memos/memo-20260527-operator-credential-models.md` (the source document for this entry — the committed two-tier plan: keyfile (free, no-org) and Workforce federation (paid, org-required), the curl-native two-leg flow, device-flow / 12-hour session cap, the per-depot mode enum homed in RBRD, and the cult-verb shape);
`Memos/memo-20260522-org-affiliated-credential-reorientation.md` (R1/R2/R3 credential model and three-tier ladder, plus R4/R5 test-rig and gauntlet resolutions);
`Memos/memo-20260427-google-native-human-auth.md` (impersonation mechanism detail — its human-auth *framing* is superseded by R2);
Google docs: Workforce Identity Federation overview (`https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/workforce-identity-federation`) and short-lived token exchange (`https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/workforce-obtaining-short-lived-credentials`).

aiTitle: Review BV mount and git commit history

content.text:
Read both. Here's the whole thing, plainly — and one honest correction I owe you.

## The imbalance (confirmed in code)

It's a textbook create/destroy asymmetry:

- **Invest** (`rbgi_add_project_iam_role`): GET project policy → add the new SA to the role → `setIamPolicy` writes the whole policy back. Touches **two** surfaces: the SA *and* the project's IAM policy.
- **Divest** (`zrbgg_divest_role`): DELETEs the service account, waits for it to vanish, removes the local key file. Touches **one** surface: the SA. **It never opens the project IAM policy.**

Deleting an SA in GCP does not remove its policy bindings — GCP just retags them `deleted:…?uid=`. So grants are `+N` per invest, revocations are `0` per divest. Monotonic, unbounded in invest/divest cycles. Two shapes feed it: fixed-name roles (director/retriever — delete+recreate same name leaves one tombstone per role per cycle) and the **datestamped governor** (a brand-new SA per mantle granted `owner`, old one orphaned — 30 mantles, 30 dead owners that can never even dedupe).

And it was **known**: `rbgi` lines 266–272 literally say repeated cycles "leave … tombstones in the policy (accepted — re-levy is quota-limited)." It was an accepted litter tradeoff. What nobody saw coming: Domain Restricted Sharing turns the litter into a wall.

## The correction I owe you

I oversold one link. I told you the tombstones are *what's blocking the director*. But `governor_mantle` and `retriever_invest` **passed in the same session**, doing `setIamPolicy` against the same bloated policy (the governor even added a fresh `owner` member). If tombstones alone tripped DRS, those should have failed too. So: **the leak is real and confirmed; "tombstones cause the director-400" is not nailed.** The director-specific cause is still open.

## What federation says about the fix

The roadmap's operator-federation tier replaces per-role *service-account keyfiles* with **federated `principal://` subjects**: keyless, the depot holds the roster, **revocation is central at the depot**. In that world there's no per-cycle SA create/delete — so no tombstones can form. The churn that leaks is a keyfile-tier artifact.

That argues against building a heavy keyfile-era binding-garbage-collector. The federation-aligned shape is **stable identity**: create each role SA once and **rotate its key** instead of delete-and-recreate (and give the governor a stable identity too). No deletes → no tombstones → and it mirrors exactly how federation will hold identity. A minimal bounded fix that federation later subsumes, rather than scaffolding it throws away.

## Sound next step

Don't fix or sweep tonight — the director-400 cause isn't pinned, so a cleanup might not even be the unblock. Let me **write this down** (the confirmed leak, the two shapes, the federation-aligned "stable-identity / key-rotate" direction, and the open director-400 cause) into the ₣BV paddock so it survives both our resets.
