rbgg_invest_retriever() {
  zrbgg_sentinel

  local z_identity="${BUZ_FOLIO:-}"

  buc_doc_brief "Invest a Retriever service account for an identity"
  buc_doc_param "identity" "Identity (required) — composes ${RBCC_account_retriever}-<identity>"
  buc_doc_shown || return 0

  test -n "${z_identity}" || buc_die "Identity required"

  local z_account_name="${RBCC_account_retriever}-${z_identity}"
  local z_account_email="${z_account_name}@${RBGD_SA_EMAIL_FULL}"

  buc_step "Investing Retriever service account: ${z_account_name}"

  zrbgg_create_service_account_with_key                                          \
    "${z_account_name}"                                                        \
    "Recipe Bottle Retriever (${z_identity})"                                  \
    "Read-only access to Google Artifact Registry - identity: ${z_identity}"   \
    "${RBCC_role_retriever}" > /dev/null || buc_die "Failed to create Retriever SA"

  local z_token
  z_token=$(rba_get_governor_token_capture) || buc_die "Failed to get admin token"

  buc_step 'Adding Artifact Registry Reader role'
  rbgi_add_project_iam_role                 \
    "${z_token}"                            \
    "Grant Artifact Registry Reader"        \
    "${RBGD_PROJECT_RESOURCE}"              \
    "${RBGC_ROLE_ARTIFACTREGISTRY_READER}"  \
    "serviceAccount:${z_account_email}"     \
    "retriever-reader"

  buc_step 'Adding Container Analysis Occurrences Viewer role'
  rbgi_add_project_iam_role                              \
    "${z_token}"                                         \
    "Grant Container Analysis Occurrences Viewer"        \
    "${RBGD_PROJECT_RESOURCE}"                           \
    "${RBGC_ROLE_CONTAINERANALYSIS_OCCURRENCES_VIEWER}"  \
    "serviceAccount:${z_account_email}"                  \
    "retriever-analysis"

  buc_info "RBRA file written: ${RBDC_ASSAY_RBRA_FILE}"
  buc_info ""
  buc_info "Move the RBRA file to a safe place — typically delivered to the"
  buc_info "operator who consumes this identity. When the invester is also the"
  buc_info "consumer, the local role slot is the canonical destination:"
  buc_bare "        mv ${RBDC_ASSAY_RBRA_FILE} ${RBDC_RETRIEVER_RBRA_FILE}"
}

rbgg_invest_director() {
  zrbgg_sentinel

  local z_identity="${BUZ_FOLIO:-}"

  buc_doc_brief "Invest a Director service account for an identity"
  buc_doc_param "identity" "Identity (required) — composes ${RBCC_account_director}-<identity>"
  buc_doc_shown || return 0

  test -n "${z_identity}" || buc_die "Identity required"

  local z_account_name="${RBCC_account_director}-${z_identity}"
  local z_account_email="${z_account_name}@${RBGD_SA_EMAIL_FULL}"

  buc_step "Investing Director service account: ${z_account_name}"

  zrbgg_create_service_account_with_key                      \
    "${z_account_name}"                                    \
    "Recipe Bottle Director (${z_identity})"               \
    "Create/destroy container images for ${z_identity}"    \
    "${RBCC_role_director}" > /dev/null || buc_die "Failed to create Director SA"

  buc_step 'Get OAuth token from admin'
  local z_token
  z_token=$(rba_get_governor_token_capture) || buc_die "Failed to get admin token"

  buc_step 'Adding Cloud Build Editor role (project scope)'
  rbgi_add_project_iam_role                 \
    "${z_token}"                            \
    "Grant Cloud Build Editor"              \
    "${RBGD_PROJECT_RESOURCE}"              \
    "${RBGC_ROLE_CLOUDBUILD_BUILDS_EDITOR}" \
    "serviceAccount:${z_account_email}"     \
    "director-cb"

  rbgi_add_project_iam_role                 \
    "${z_token}"                            \
    "Grant Project Viewer"                  \
    "${RBGD_PROJECT_RESOURCE}"              \
    "roles/viewer"                          \
    "serviceAccount:${z_account_email}"     \
    "director-viewer"

  rbgi_add_project_iam_role                 \
    "${z_token}"                            \
    "Grant Worker Pool User"               \
    "${RBGD_PROJECT_RESOURCE}"              \
    "roles/cloudbuild.workerPoolUser"       \
    "serviceAccount:${z_account_email}"     \
    "director-pool"

  buc_step 'Grant serviceAccountUser on Mason'
  rbgi_add_sa_iam_role "${z_token}" "${RBGD_MASON_EMAIL}" "${z_account_email}" "roles/iam.serviceAccountUser"

  buc_step 'Grant Artifact Registry roles (complete expected policy)'
  # Complete policy: Director repoAdmin + Mason writer in one setIamPolicy.
  # Prevents read-modify-write race where stale getIamPolicy omits Mason's binding.
  local -r z_gar_resource="projects/${RBGD_GAR_PROJECT_ID}/locations/${RBGD_GAR_LOCATION}/repositories/${RBDC_GAR_REPOSITORY}"
  local -r z_gar_get_url="${RBGC_API_ROOT_ARTIFACTREGISTRY}${RBGC_ARTIFACTREGISTRY_V1}/${z_gar_resource}:getIamPolicy?options.requestedPolicyVersion=3"
  local -r z_gar_set_url="${RBGC_API_ROOT_ARTIFACTREGISTRY}${RBGC_ARTIFACTREGISTRY_V1}/${z_gar_resource}:setIamPolicy"

  # Propagation retry — AR repo is resource-scope: member-visibility 400s plus
  # caller-recently-empowered 403 from the resource-scope IAM cache (the
  # governor's roles/owner grant may not yet have reached the AR cache).
  local -ra z_gar_tolerance=(
    "400" "*does not exist*"
    "400" "*is not deleted*"
    "403" ""
  )
  local z_gar_prop_delay=${RBGC_PROPAGATION_INITIAL_DELAY_SEC}
  local z_gar_prop_elapsed=0
  local -r z_gar_prop_deadline=${RBGC_PROPAGATION_DEADLINE_SEC}
  local z_gar_prop_attempt=0
  local z_gar_get_infix=""
  local z_gar_set_infix=""
  local z_gar_get_code=""

  while :; do
    z_gar_prop_attempt=$((z_gar_prop_attempt + 1))
    z_gar_get_infix="director_gar_get_iam-${z_gar_prop_elapsed}s"
    z_gar_set_infix="director_gar_set_iam-${z_gar_prop_elapsed}s"

    rbuh_json "GET" "${z_gar_get_url}" "${z_token}" "${z_gar_get_infix}"
    z_gar_get_code=$(rbuh_code_capture "${z_gar_get_infix}") || z_gar_get_code=""

    # Propagation retry on GET — covers newly-empowered governor (403) and
    # newly-created Director SA member-visibility lag (400 patterns).
    if zrbgi_propagation_error_predicate "${z_gar_get_infix}" "${z_gar_get_code}" "${z_gar_tolerance[@]}"; then
      test "${z_gar_prop_elapsed}" -lt "${z_gar_prop_deadline}" \
        || buc_die "GAR IAM: propagation timeout after ${z_gar_prop_elapsed}s"
      buc_log_args "GAR getIamPolicy returned ${z_gar_get_code} (propagation delay; attempt ${z_gar_prop_attempt}, ${z_gar_prop_elapsed}s)"
      sleep "${z_gar_prop_delay}"
      z_gar_prop_elapsed=$((z_gar_prop_elapsed + z_gar_prop_delay))
      z_gar_prop_delay=$((z_gar_prop_delay * 2))
      test "${z_gar_prop_delay}" -le "${RBGC_PROPAGATION_MAX_DELAY_SEC}" || z_gar_prop_delay=${RBGC_PROPAGATION_MAX_DELAY_SEC}
      continue
    fi

    rbuh_require_ok "Get GAR repo IAM policy" "${z_gar_get_infix}"

    # Build complete expected policy: Director repoAdmin + Mason writer
    local z_gar_partial
    z_gar_partial=$(rbgi_jq_add_member_to_role_capture "${z_gar_get_infix}" \
      "roles/artifactregistry.repoAdmin" "serviceAccount:${z_account_email}" "") \
      || buc_die "Failed to add Director repoAdmin to GAR IAM policy"

    local z_gar_intermediate="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/rbuh_director_gar_complete_iam_u_resp.json"
    printf '%s\n' "${z_gar_partial}" > "${z_gar_intermediate}" \
      || buc_die "Failed to write intermediate GAR IAM policy"

    local z_gar_complete
    z_gar_complete=$(rbgi_jq_add_member_to_role_capture "director_gar_complete_iam" \
      "roles/artifactregistry.writer" "serviceAccount:${RBGD_MASON_EMAIL}" "") \
      || buc_die "Failed to add Mason writer to GAR IAM policy"

    local z_gar_set_body="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/rbgg_gar_complete_policy_body.json"
    printf '{"policy":%s}\n' "${z_gar_complete}" > "${z_gar_set_body}" \
      || buc_die "Failed to write GAR setIamPolicy body"
    rbuh_json "POST" "${z_gar_set_url}" "${z_token}" "${z_gar_set_infix}" "${z_gar_set_body}"

    local z_gar_set_code
    z_gar_set_code=$(rbuh_code_capture "${z_gar_set_infix}") || buc_die "No HTTP code from GAR setIamPolicy"

    # Propagation retry on SET — same tolerance list as GET.
    if zrbgi_propagation_error_predicate "${z_gar_set_infix}" "${z_gar_set_code}" "${z_gar_tolerance[@]}"; then
      test "${z_gar_prop_elapsed}" -lt "${z_gar_prop_deadline}" \
        || buc_die "GAR IAM: propagation timeout after ${z_gar_prop_elapsed}s"
      buc_log_args "GAR setIamPolicy returned ${z_gar_set_code} (propagation delay; attempt ${z_gar_prop_attempt}, ${z_gar_prop_elapsed}s)"
      sleep "${z_gar_prop_delay}"
      z_gar_prop_elapsed=$((z_gar_prop_elapsed + z_gar_prop_delay))
      z_gar_prop_delay=$((z_gar_prop_delay * 2))
      test "${z_gar_prop_delay}" -le "${RBGC_PROPAGATION_MAX_DELAY_SEC}" || z_gar_prop_delay=${RBGC_PROPAGATION_MAX_DELAY_SEC}
      continue
    fi

    rbuh_require_ok "Set GAR repo IAM policy (complete)" "${z_gar_set_infix}"
    break
  done

  buc_info "RBRA file written: ${RBDC_ASSAY_RBRA_FILE}"
  buc_info ""
  buc_info "Move the RBRA file to a safe place — typically delivered to the"
  buc_info "operator who consumes this identity. When the invester is also the"
  buc_info "consumer, the local role slot is the canonical destination:"
  buc_bare "        mv ${RBDC_ASSAY_RBRA_FILE} ${RBDC_DIRECTOR_RBRA_FILE}"
}

# Divest a single role+identity: synthesize email, DELETE (404-tolerant),
# opportunistic role-RBRA cleanup only on email match.

aiTitle: Review BV mount and git commit history

content.text:
The code read sharpens the puzzle rather than resolving it — and that's important. **Both** retriever and director grant at *project* scope via the same `rbgi_add_project_iam_role` → GET-policy → add-member → `setIamPolicy`. So scope doesn't explain the asymmetry. The sequence was: retriever grants `artifactregistry.reader` + `containeranalysis.occurrences.viewer` (both **passed**), then director's *first* grant `cloudbuild.builds.editor` (**failed**). Same mechanism, same bloated policy, same session — so the failure is specific to that one binding/role, not "the policy has tombstones" (or retriever's grants would've failed too).

That reframes the decisive question: **what's different about the `cloudbuild.builds.editor` binding?** Let me look per-binding — non-mutating — for a member unique to it.

Structurally the policy is clean — version 1, no conditions, no duplicate role rows, 21 KB. So the failed and passed bindings are **observationally identical except the role name and which dead-SA type fills the tombstones**. I genuinely can't pin *why* `cloudbuild.builds.editor` rejects while `artifactregistry.reader` accepts without a controlled write — the observation has hit its floor.

Let me finish the one remaining non-mutating thread first — the **blast radius**: the same divest-never-revokes pattern should be leaking on the GAR repo and the Mason SA too, not just the project.

content.text:
Confirmed — the leak is **not** project-only. Same divest-never-revokes pattern, three resources:

| Resource | deleted | live |
|---|---|---|
| Project IAM policy | 145 (76 distinct SAs) | 8 |
| GAR repo policy | 23 | 1 (mason) |
| Mason SA policy | 23 | 1 |

Every resource the invest path grants on accumulates tombstones, because `zrbgg_divest_role` (rbgg 772–817) only DELETEs the SA — it never opens *any* of those policies to remove the binding first.

## The leak — fully characterized (memo-ready)

- **Mechanism:** invest = create SA + `setIamPolicy` grant (read-modify-write, `rbgi_add_project_iam_role` 139–275). divest = delete SA only. Deleting a principal doesn't remove its bindings — GCP retags them `deleted:…?uid=`. So grants are `+N`/cycle, revocations `0`/cycle. Monotonic.
- **Confirmed in code, and already known:** `rbgi` 266–272 literally calls the tombstones "accepted — re-levy is quota-limited." The accepted litter just turned load-bearing.
- **Two shapes:** fixed-name roles (director/retriever — one tombstone per role per cycle) and the **datestamped governor** (a fresh SA per mantle granted `owner`; 30 dead owners that can never dedupe).
- **Rate & ceiling:** ~6 project tombstones per `canonical-invest` run + GAR/Mason. Independent of DRS, this marches toward GCP's ~1500-member policy cap — a hard wall even if the org policy didn't exist. DRS just made it bite at ~150.

## The director-400 — still unexplained, and I won't pretend otherwise

Observation has bottomed out. The failed binding (`cloudbuild.builds.editor`) and the passing ones (`artifactregistry.reader`, the GAR repo, Mason SA) are identical in every measurable dimension — same submit shape (23 dead + 1 new in-domain SA), no conditions, no duplicate rows, v1 policy. Both retriever and director add a freshly-created in-domain SA at project scope via the same code. One passes, one fails. Under *whole-policy* DRS they'd both fail; under *delta* DRS they'd both pass. Neither model fits.

My leading hypothesis (testable): **a DRS propagation race on the just-created director SA** — DRS can't yet confirm the brand-new SA belongs to customer `C02ystilf`, so it rejects. Crucially, `rbgi`'s retry tolerance covers `"does not exist"` / `"is not deleted"` 400s but **not** the "incompatible with org policy" 400 — so this one dies on the first try instead of being waited out. That would also explain why it's intermittent and why past ordains "worked." But it's a hypothesis; the retriever-passed-same-session fact still nags.

## Experiments to pin it (these need a write — your call)

1. **Idempotent resubmit** (near-zero risk): GET the policy, `setIamPolicy` it back unchanged. 400 → the whole policy is now unwritable; 200 → the trigger is adding to that specific binding. Decisive and reversible (no semantic change).
2. **Role isolation:** add a *known-good, long-existing* in-domain member (e.g. the live retriever) to `cloudbuild.builds.editor`. Fails → role/binding-specific; passes → it's the *freshly-created* director SA (supports the race).
3. **Cleanup = the fix:** strip all `deleted:` members, `setIamPolicy`. Confirms remediation and unblocks the director in one move.

One non-mutating add-on I can do anytime: pull Google's documented DRS evaluation semantics (whole-policy vs delta; new-principal propagation) to ground hypothesis vs fact.

## Memo

I can draft `Memos/memo-20260604-iam-divest-binding-leak.md` now — it captures the phenomenon you want regardless of the director-400 resolution: mechanism, the two shapes, three-resource blast radius, growth-to-hard-cap, the federation-aligned fix direction (stable identity / key-rotate, not keyfile-era binding-GC), and the director-400 as a flagged open question.

You know this org/DRS setup better than I can reverse-engineer it — does the `cloudbuild.builds.editor`-specific failure ring any bells? And shall I (a) draft the memo now, (b) run experiment 1 (the safe idempotent probe) first, or both?

content.text: Authorized — let me run the decisive battery. The cleanest controlled test: **(A)** resubmit the whole policy unchanged (is it writable as-is under DRS?), and **(B′)** grant `cloudbuild.builds.editor` to the director SA *that already exists from yesterday's run* — now fully propagated. If a propagated SA still rejects, it's deterministic (not a freshness race); if it now succeeds, freshness was the variable and the original failure was transient. Running that, and loading WebSearch in parallel.

input.command:
set +x
base=/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_beta_recipemuster/../station-files/secrets
CS="$base/client_secrets/client_secret_727189059962-ddv3midird5bvukmca6na61d2r8d4i4j.apps.googleusercontent.com.json"
CID=$(jq -r '(.installed//.web).client_id' "$CS")
. "$base/payor/rbro.env"
TOK=$(curl -s -X POST https://oauth2.googleapis.com/token -d client_id="$CID" -d client_secret="$RBRO_CLIENT_SECRET" -d refresh_token="$RBRO_REFRESH_TOKEN" -d grant_type=refresh_token | jq -r '.access_token // empty')
[ -z "$TOK" ] && { echo "token mint failed"; exit 1; }
export CLOUDSDK_AUTH_ACCESS_TOKEN="$TOK"
P=cancbhm-d-canest3bhm100001
DIR="director-canest-dir@${P}.iam.gserviceaccount.com"

echo "=== does the director SA from yesterday still exist? ==="
gcloud iam service-accounts describe "$DIR" --project="$P" --format='value(email,disabled)' 2>&1 | head -3

echo
echo "=== EXPERIMENT A: resubmit the whole (tombstoned) policy UNCHANGED ==="
if gcloud projects get-iam-policy "$P" --format=json > /tmp/rbpol.json 2>/tmp/rbgeterr.txt; then
  echo "GET ok ($(wc -c </tmp/rbpol.json) bytes, deleted=$(jq '[.bindings[].members[]|select(startswith("deleted:"))]|length' /tmp/rbpol.json))"
  if gcloud projects set-iam-policy "$P" /tmp/rbpol.json --format=none >/tmp/rbset.txt 2>&1; then
    echo "A RESULT => SUCCEEDED: the whole tombstoned policy IS writable as-is"
  else
    echo "A RESULT => FAILED:"; tail -6 /tmp/rbset.txt
  fi
else
  echo "GET failed:"; cat /tmp/rbgeterr.txt
fi

echo
echo "=== EXPERIMENT B': grant cloudbuild.builds.editor to the now-propagated director SA ==="
if gcloud projects add-iam-policy-binding "$P" --member="serviceAccount:${DIR}" --role=roles/cloudbuild.builds.editor --condition=None >/tmp/rbb.txt 2>&1; then
  echo "B' RESULT => SUCCEEDED: grant went through (original failure was transient / freshness)"
else
  echo "B' RESULT => FAILED:"; tail -8 /tmp/rbb.txt
fi

content.text:
Both experiments succeeded — and that **reframes the whole thing honestly**:

- **(A)** The full 145-tombstone policy resubmits cleanly. So tombstones do **not** block `setIamPolicy` and are **not** DRS-rejected. That overturns yesterday's central guess.
- **(B′)** Granting `cloudbuild.builds.editor` to the *now-propagated* director SA succeeds.

So the leak and the director-400 are **two separate things**: the leak is real but (so far) benign; the director-400 was almost certainly a **DRS propagation race on the freshly-created SA** — and `rbgi`'s retry tolerance doesn't list that 400, so it died on the first try instead of waiting it out.

Your call is the right confirmation: `dogfight` reruns the *real* director invest (fresh SA → immediate grant) end-to-end. If it passes, the race just didn't recur (timing); if it fails again on the fresh SA while my propagated-SA grant passed, the race is confirmed. Launching it, plus the WebSearch to ground the DRS semantics, and a tree check (dogfight refuses a dirty tree).

Based on the search results, I found relevant information about the `iam.allowedPolicyMemberDomains` organization policy constraint and issues related to `setIamPolicy` with a 400 error.

## Key Findings:

**Propagation Delay for Newly Created Service Accounts:**

If the `iam.allowedPolicyMemberDomains` organization policy constraint is enforced in your organization, then you might not be able to grant roles to newly created groups. If you get a failedPrecondition error when trying to grant a role to a newly created group, wait 24 hours, and then try granting the role again.

More generally, policy changes take effect within 2 minutes. However, in some cases, it can take 7 minutes or more for changes to propagate across the system.

**400 Error Context:**

The search results show that when the `iam.allowedPolicyMemberDomains` constraint is violated during a `setIamPolicy` operation, when the constraint is violated by trying to add a principal that is not included in the allowed_values list, the operation will fail and then an error message will be displayed with "FAILED_PRECONDITION: One or more users named in the policy do not belong to a permitted customer."

This suggests that newly created service accounts may need time to propagate through the system before they can be properly recognized by the organization policy validation, which can cause 400-level errors on `setIamPolicy` calls.

content.text:
The WebSearch **confirms the mechanism** from Google's own docs:

> "If the `iam.allowedPolicyMemberDomains` constraint is enforced… you might not be able to grant roles to newly created [identities]. If you get a failedPrecondition error when granting a role to a newly created [principal], wait… and try again. Policy changes take effect within 2 minutes; in some cases 7 minutes or more to propagate."

That's the freshness race exactly: a just-created SA isn't yet recognized as belonging to customer `C02ystilf`, so DRS rejects the grant — and `rbgi`'s retry tolerance covers `"does not exist"`/`"is not deleted"` 400s but **not** this org-policy 400, so it dies on the first attempt. My experiments bracket it cleanly: propagated SA → grant succeeds (B′); fresh SA yesterday → rejected. And it explains why canonical-invest passes on cerebro/cygwin but lost the race on macOS — it's **timing, server-side, host-independent**, not platform-specific.

**The picture is now firm — two independent findings:**

1. **Director-invest DRS propagation race** — the actual thing that blocked blockade. Intermittent. Fix: add the org-policy 400 (`"incompatible with the provided policy"` / `"do not belong to a permitted customer"`) to `rbgi`'s propagation-retry tolerance, deadline sized to Google's documented window (minutes, up to ~7+).
2. **The tombstone leak** — real, unbounded, ≥3 resources — but Experiment A proved it does **not** cause the 400 (the 145-tombstone policy resubmits fine). It's the deeper phenomenon, separate from the blocker.

`dogfight` is running (`bc26dsy2n`) — it reruns the fresh director invest end-to-end. Two informative outcomes: **fails again** → the race robustly reproduces (fresh SA, immediate grant) and the retry-tolerance fix is clearly warranted; **passes** → it won the race this time, confirming intermittency. Either firms the diagnosis. Waiting on it.

Sources:
- [Restrict identities with domain-restricted sharing — Google Cloud](https://cloud.google.com/resource-manager/docs/organization-policy/restricting-domains)
- [Troubleshoot organization policy errors for service accounts — Google Cloud IAM](https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/troubleshoot-org-policies)

content.text:
Confirmed: **director-canest-dir is now gone** (the divest *did* delete it; `--show-deleted` count 0, so not even soft-delete-reserved), while the retriever it re-made is live. So the "already exists" was a **transient delete→create race**: the divest's gone-poll said clear, but the invest's create-preflight read still saw the SA for a moment. Same eventual-consistency class as the DRS race, different edge — and retriever won that race this run while director lost it.

## The full picture — one root, three faces

Everything we've found traces to **the keyfile tier's per-cycle SA create/delete/grant pattern** colliding with GCP's eventual consistency and the org's DRS:

1. **Binding tombstone leak** — divest deletes the SA but never revokes its policy bindings, so `deleted:…?uid=` entries accumulate, unbounded, on every resource the invest grants (project + GAR repo + Mason SA). Experiment A proved this is *benign so far* — the 145-tombstone policy resubmits fine — but it marches toward GCP's ~1500-member cap.
2. **create→grant DRS race** — a freshly-minted SA isn't yet recognized as belonging to customer `C02ystilf`, so DRS rejects the grant (400), and `rbgi`'s retry tolerance doesn't cover that error. *This blocked blockade.* (Confirmed: propagated SA grants fine via B′; Google docs say up to 7 min.)
3. **delete→create race** — divest reports gone, immediate re-create sees it still there → false "already exists." *This blocked dogfight today.*

All three intermittent, all host-independent (they pass on cerebro/cygwin and lost on macOS purely on timing), all symptoms of churning SAs every cycle.

**Federation is the unifying fix, not a coincidence.** The operator-federation tier grants capabilities to stable `principal://` subjects with central revocation — **no per-cycle SA create/delete/grant at all**. That single change dissolves *all three*: no churn → no tombstones, no fresh-SA DRS race, no delete/create race. The keyfile-tier interim patches (divest-revokes-first or stable-identity/key-rotate for the leak; add the DRS 400 to `rbgi`'s retry tolerance for race 2; harden the divest→create handoff for race 3) are stopgaps federation later subsumes — which is exactly why we shouldn't over-build them.

**Heat status:** macOS blockade is still blocked — but by these lifecycle races, *not* anything macOS-specific. siege stays green/banked.

## I think we're done characterizing — time to write it

We have the mechanism, the blast radius, the benign-vs-fatal distinction (A), the race confirmations (B′, dogfight, Google docs), and the federation-aligned fix direction. Further dogfight runs would just replay intermittent races (and re-mantle the shared governor again).

Proposed: `Memos/memo-20260604-credential-churn-leak-and-propagation-races.md`, structured as —
1. **Phenomenon** — per-cycle SA churn as the root
2. **Three faces** — tombstone leak; create→grant DRS race; delete→create race (with the exact evidence/experiments for each)
3. **Why it surfaced on macOS** — timing, not platform
4. **Fix direction** — federation as the dissolver; keyfile-tier stopgaps and their retirement condition
5. **Open/secondary** — the asymmetry nuances we didn't fully close

Want me to draft it on that skeleton, or adjust the framing first?

```
# First ifrit run on uncontrolled Cygwin — siege 45/46 and the conntrack_spoofed_ack breach

*2026-06-03. Heat ₣BV (cygwin-spike). This was the **first time the ifrit
adversarial security suite ran on an uncontrolled Cygwin + Docker Desktop
host** (`cygwin@rocket`).*

## Headline

The `siege` suite (renamed this session from `tadmor` = `kludge-tadmor` +
`tadmor` crucible fixture) charged on Cygwin Docker Desktop and ran the full
adversarial sortie set: **45 passed, 1 failed (46 total)**, clean quench.
That is the heat's headline essentially achieved — theurge's security suite
runs, and 45 escape vectors are correctly contained, on a box we do not
provision.

The single failure is recorded below. It does not diminish the result, but it
is a genuine finding that needs adjudication, **not** dismissal.

## The one breach

```
FAILED: rbtdrc_sortie_conntrack_spoofed_ack
BREACH: spoofed ACK to 192.0.46.9:80 got response —
        conntrack RELATED,ESTABLISHED not filtering stateless ACKs
IFRIT_VERDICT: FAIL BREACH ...
```

Sortie: `rbtdrc_sortie_conntrack_spoofed_ack` →
`rbtdrc_invoke_ifrit(ctx, "conntrack-spoofed-ack", ...)` — an ifrit sortie
driven by the `rbtid` adjutant. (Full packet/source construction lives in the
sortie script; not yet read — see Next Steps.)

Sentry FORWARD chain at the time (from the captured `iptables-rules.txt`):

```
-P FORWARD DROP
-A FORWARD -m state --state RELATED,ESTABLISHED -j ACCEPT
-A FORWARD -j RBM-FORWARD
...
-A RBM-FORWARD -d 192.0.32.0/20 -i eth0 -j ACCEPT      # allowlisted CIDR

input.content:
# Credential-lifecycle churn: one binding leak and two propagation races

*2026-06-04. Heat ₣BV. Surfaced while bringing the `blockade` (moriah / airgap)
crucible up on macOS Docker Desktop, branch
`bv-macos-crucible-20260603-BVAAE`. The depot under study is the canonical
standing depot `cancbhm-d-canest3bhm100001` (org `247899326218`), the same
shared depot cerebro and `cygwin@rocket` re-credential against.*

## Headline

The per-role service-account **create / delete / grant-every-cycle** pattern of
the keyfile credential tier collides with two unforgiving realities of the
Google substrate — IAM eventual consistency and the org's Domain Restricted
Sharing policy — and it also leaks IAM bindings without bound. One root,
**three distinct faces**:

1. **Binding tombstone leak** — every invest grants a binding; no divest ever
   revokes one. Accumulates `deleted:…?uid=` members without bound on every
   resource the invest touches. *Real, unbounded, currently benign.*
2. **create→grant DRS race** — a freshly-minted SA is not yet recognized as
   belonging to the allowed customer, so the role grant is rejected by Domain
   Restricted Sharing, and the rejection is not retried. *This is what blocked
   `blockade`.*
3. **delete→create race** — a divest reports the SA gone, but the immediately
   following re-create still sees it, and fails "already exists." *This is what
   blocked `dogfight` the next morning.*

All three are **host-independent and intermittent**. canonical-invest passes
routinely on cerebro and Cygwin; on macOS it lost race (2) on 06-03 and race
(3) on 06-04. Nothing here is macOS-specific — macOS was just where the dice
came up cold two days running. `siege` (tether crucible, fully local, no GCP)
is green on macOS and unaffected.

The unifying fix is **operator federation** (RBSHR Horizon entry; source memo
`memo-20260527-operator-credential-models.md`): granting capabilities to stable
`principal://` subjects with central revocation removes per-cycle SA
create/delete/grant entirely — which dissolves all three faces at once. The
keyfile-tier patches below are stopgaps that federation later subsumes, and
should not be over-engineered.

## Setting: the org policy and the polluted policy

The org enforces `constraints/iam.allowedPolicyMemberDomains` (Domain
Restricted Sharing, "DRS"), allow-listing exactly one customer:

```
constraint: constraints/iam.allowedPolicyMemberDomains
listPolicy:
  allowedValues:
  - C02ystilf            # scaleinvariant.org's Cloud Identity customer
```

This is a normal, protective policy — not hostile, not recent. The only live
human principal in the project policy is `user:bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org`
(in-domain); the rest are in-project service accounts and DRS-exempt Google
service agents.

The project IAM policy at time of study (`gcloud projects get-iam-policy`):

```
157 member entries, 86 distinct
145 are deleted:serviceAccount:…?uid= tombstones (76 distinct dead SAs)
  director-canest-dir   × 69     (across cloudbuild.builds.editor,
                                  cloudbuild.workerPoolUser, viewer)
  retriever-canest-ret  × 46     (artifactregistry.reader,
                                  containeranalysis.occurrences.viewer)
  governor-YYYYMMDDHHMM × 30     (all in roles/owner, never dedupe)
```

The same leak is present on the **GAR repo policy** (23 dead) and the **Mason
SA policy** (23 dead): every resource the invest path grants on accumulates.

## Face 1 — the binding tombstone leak

**Mechanism (confirmed in code).** Invest and divest are asymmetric:

- `rbgi_add_project_iam_role` (and its repo / SA / bucket / secret siblings):
  GET policy → add the new member to the role binding → `setIamPolicy` writes
  the whole policy back. The grant touches **two** surfaces: the SA *and* the
  resource's IAM policy.
- `zrbgg_divest_role` (`rbgg_Governor.sh`): DELETEs the SA, polls until the GET
  reports it gone, removes the local RBRA file. It **never opens any IAM
  policy.** Deleting an SA does not remove its bindings — GCP retags them
  `deleted:…?uid=` and leaves them in place forever.

So grants are `+N` per invest, revocations `0` per divest: monotonic, unbounded
in the number of invest/divest cycles. Two accrual shapes — fixed-name roles
(director/retriever leave one tombstone per role per cycle) and the
**datestamped governor** (each mantle is a brand-new SA name granted `owner`;
the 30 dead owners can never even dedupe).

This was **already known and accepted**: `rbgi_add_project_iam_role` carries a
comment stating that repeated divest/invest cycles "leave same-email
`deleted:serviceAccount:…?uid=` tombstones in the policy (accepted — re-levy is
quota-limited)." The accepted litter has not yet turned load-bearing, but it is
unbounded.

**It is benign so far — proven, not assumed.** *Experiment A:* GET the full
145-tombstone project policy and `setIamPolicy` it back unchanged →
**HTTP 200.** The tombstoned policy is fully writable; DRS does not reject the
`deleted:` members on resubmission. So the leak does **not** cause any of the
grant failures below. Its real ceiling is GCP's ~1500-member allow-policy cap,
which the depot marches toward at ~6 project tombstones per `canonical-invest`
run (plus GAR/Mason) — a hard wall that would arrive **independent of DRS**.

## Face 2 — the create→grant DRS race (blocked `blockade`)

**What happened (06-03).** `blockade`, made self-credentialing by prepending
`canonical-invest`, ran governor-mantle ✓, retriever-invest ✓, then
director-invest failed at its first grant:

```
rbgg_invest_director: Service account created: director-canest-dir@…  (ok)
rbgg_invest_director: Adding Cloud Build Editor role (project scope)
ERROR: Grant Cloud Build Editor (set policy) (HTTP 400):
       This resource is under organization policy controls which are
       incompatible with the provided policy.
```
(durable: `logs-buk/hist-rbw-adI-sh-20260603-103842-26773-301.txt`)

**Mechanism.** The grant member is a service account created *seconds earlier*.
DRS validates that each member belongs to customer `C02ystilf`; a brand-new SA
is not yet recognized as in-customer, so DRS rejects the grant. Google
documents exactly this: when `iam.allowedPolicyMemberDomains` is enforced, role
grants to newly-created principals can fail until membership propagates —
typically ~2 minutes, "in some cases 7 minutes or more" (groups up to 24h).
[restricting-domains], [troubleshoot-org-policies]

**Why it is fatal rather than slow.** `rbgi_add_project_iam_role`'s
propagation-retry tolerance covers `400 "*does not exist*"` and
`400 "*is not deleted*"`, but **not** the DRS "incompatible with the provided
policy" 400. So this rejection dies on the first attempt instead of being
waited out like every other propagation lag the function already handles.

**Confirmation.** *Experiment B′:* grant `roles/cloudbuild.builds.editor` to the
*same* director SA the day after it was created — now fully propagated →
**HTTP 200.** Freshness was the only variable. (Combined with Experiment A,
this rules out the tombstones and isolates new-principal propagation as the
cause.)

## Face 3 — the delete→create race (blocked `dogfight`)

**What happened (06-04).** Re-running the real path via `dogfight`
(canonical-invest + dogfight): governor-mantle ✓, retriever-invest ✓,
director-invest failed — but this time *before* any grant:

```
ERROR: Service account director-canest-dir@… already exists —
       run the matching GovernorDivests verb (rbw-adD) first to re-key
```
(durable: `logs-buk/hist-rbw-adI-sh-20260604-092730-7113-822.txt`;
suite `logs-buk/hist-rbw-ts-dogfight-20260604-092601-3729-496.txt`)

**Mechanism.** `rbtdrk_role_invest_impl` divests-then-invests for both roles
specifically to make standing-depot reruns race-free. The director divest ran
and reported "Divest operation completed" — yet the invest's create-preflight
(which refuses to overwrite an existing SA) immediately saw the SA as still
present. The divest's gone-confirmation and the create-preflight read a
not-yet-converged view of the same SA. A *state check minutes later* showed
`director-canest-dir` fully gone, with **no** soft-delete reservation
(`--show-deleted` count 0) — i.e. the deletion was real; the create-preflight
simply read stale. The retriever, divested+recreated in the same run, won the
same race; the director lost it.

This is the same eventual-consistency class as Face 2, on a different edge
(delete→read rather than create→validate).

## Why it presented as a macOS problem (it isn't)

DRS validation and SA delete/create propagation are server-side and
host-independent. canonical-invest passes routinely on cerebro and
`cygwin@rocket` because those runs won the races; the two macOS runs lost one
race each on consecutive days. There is **no macOS-specific defect** in either
face. The macOS crucible itself is sound: `siege` (local kludge build + tether
charge + full ifrit sortie set) is 60/60 green on macOS Docker Desktop,
including `conntrack_spoofed_ack` (which native Linux passes and Windows DD
breaches — a separate ₣BV finding).

## Fix direction

**Federation dissolves all three.** The roadmap's operator-federation tier
(RBSHR; `memo-20260527-operator-credential-models.md`) grants capabilities to
stable external-IdP `principal://` subjects with revocation central at the
depot. There is no per-cycle SA create/delete/grant — so no binding to leak
(Face 1), no fresh SA to fail DRS validation (Face 2), and no delete/create
edge to race (Face 3). The three faces are all artifacts of churning a
short-lived SA every cycle; the federation model simply does not churn.

**Keyfile-tier stopgaps** (interim; retire when federation lands or when the
keyfile tier is deprecated):

- *Face 2:* add the DRS org-policy 400 (`"incompatible with the provided
  policy"` / `"do not belong to a permitted customer"`) to
  `rbgi_add_project_iam_role`'s propagation-retry tolerance, with a deadline
  matching Google's documented window (minutes; budget ≥7). This is the
  smallest fix that unblocks `blockade`/`skirmish`/`dogfight` reliably.
- *Face 3:* harden the divest→invest handoff so the create-preflight tolerates
  a not-yet-propagated prior deletion (retry the "already exists" preflight, or
  have the create share the divest's gone-confirmation path) rather than failing
  hard.
- *Face 1:* either make divest revoke bindings before deleting the SA (mirror
  the grant), or — better aligned with federation — stop churning: invest once
  on a **stable** identity and **rotate the key** instead of delete-and-recreate
  (the governor's datestamped-per-mantle shape is the worst offender and the
  clearest candidate to make stable). Independently, a one-time sweep of the
  existing 145 project tombstones (+ GAR repo + Mason SA) clears the current
  pollution; it is safe (touches only already-deleted principals) and was shown
  writable by Experiment A.

The principle (CMK "load-bearing complexity" / the Pale): DRS and GCP eventual
consistency are a neighbor's realm — we conduct ourselves against them
(characterize, retry the surveyed signature, log the bend) rather than legislate
them. The stopgaps above are membranes with a retirement condition
(federation), not permanent structure.

## Open / secondary

- The per-role asymmetry within a single run (retriever grant/recreate succeeds
  while director fails) is fully consistent with intermittent races but was not
  independently proven per-role; it needs no further chase unless a
  *deterministic* per-role difference reappears.
- Whether DRS evaluates the whole submitted policy or only the changed members
  was not settled directly; Experiment A (whole tombstoned policy resubmits
  clean) shows that, whichever it is, existing `deleted:` members are not the
  trigger.

## Evidence appendix

- DRS / policy state: `gcloud resource-manager org-policies …`,
  `gcloud projects get-iam-policy cancbhm-d-canest3bhm100001` (payor-token
  authenticated, 2026-06-04).
- Experiment A: `set-iam-policy` of the unchanged 145-tombstone policy → 200.
- Experiment B′: `add-iam-policy-binding … roles/cloudbuild.builds.editor` to
  the day-old director SA → 200.
- Face 2 original: `logs-buk/hist-rbw-adI-sh-20260603-103842-26773-301.txt`.
- Face 3 rerun: `logs-buk/hist-rbw-adI-sh-20260604-092730-7113-822.txt`,
  `logs-buk/hist-rbw-ts-dogfight-20260604-092601-3729-496.txt`.
- Code: `rbgi_add_project_iam_role` and the accepted-tombstone comment
  (`Tools/rbk/rbgi_IAM.sh`); `zrbgg_divest_role`, `rbgg_invest_director`,
  `rbgg_invest_retriever` (`Tools/rbk/rbgg_Governor.sh`);
  `rbtdrk_role_invest_impl` (`Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrk_canonical.rs`).
- Google docs: Restrict identities with domain-restricted sharing; Troubleshoot
  organization policy errors for service accounts.

[restricting-domains]: https://cloud.google.com/resource-manager/docs/organization-policy/restricting-domains
[troubleshoot-org-policies]: https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/troubleshoot-org-policies

145-tombstone project policy and `setIamPolicy` it back unchanged →
**HTTP 200.** The tombstoned policy is fully writable; DRS does not reject the
`deleted:` members on resubmission. So the leak does **not** cause any of the
grant failures below. Its real ceiling is GCP's ~1500-member allow-policy cap,
which the depot marches toward at ~6 project tombstones per `canonical-invest`
run (plus GAR/Mason) — a hard wall that would arrive **independent of DRS**.

## Face 2 — the create→grant DRS race (blocked `blockade`)

**What happened (06-03).** `blockade`, made self-credentialing by prepending
`canonical-invest`, ran governor-mantle ✓, retriever-invest ✓, then
director-invest failed at its first grant:

```
rbgg_invest_director: Service account created: director-canest-dir@…  (ok)
rbgg_invest_director: Adding Cloud Build Editor role (project scope)
ERROR: Grant Cloud Build Editor (set policy) (HTTP 400):
       This resource is under organization policy controls which are
       incompatible with the provided policy.
```
(durable: `logs-buk/hist-rbw-adI-sh-20260603-103842-26773-301.txt`)

**Mechanism.** The grant member is a service account created *seconds earlier*.
DRS validates that each member belongs to customer `C02ystilf`; a brand-new SA
is not yet recognized as in-customer, so DRS rejects the grant. Google
documents exactly this: when `iam.allowedPolicyMemberDomains` is enforced, role
grants to newly-created principals can fail until membership propagates —
typically ~2 minutes, "in some cases 7 minutes or more" (groups up to 24h).
[restricting-domains], [troubleshoot-org-policies]

**Why it is fatal rather than slow.** `rbgi_add_project_iam_role`'s
propagation-retry tolerance covers `400 "*does not exist*"` and
`400 "*is not deleted*"`, but **not** the DRS "incompatible with the provided
policy" 400. So this rejection dies on the first attempt instead of being
waited out like every other propagation lag the function already handles.

**Confirmation.** *Experiment B′:* grant `roles/cloudbuild.builds.editor` to the
*same* director SA the day after it was created — now fully propagated →
**HTTP 200.** Freshness was the only variable. (Combined with Experiment A,
this rules out the tombstones and isolates new-principal propagation as the
cause.)

## Face 3 — the delete→create race (blocked `dogfight`)

**What happened (06-04).** Re-running the real path via `dogfight`
(canonical-invest + dogfight): governor-mantle ✓, retriever-invest ✓,
director-invest failed — but this time *before* any grant:

```
ERROR: Service account director-canest-dir@… already exists —
       run the matching GovernorDivests verb (rbw-adD) first to re-key
```
(durable: `logs-buk/hist-rbw-adI-sh-20260604-092730-7113-822.txt`;
suite `logs-buk/hist-rbw-ts-dogfight-20260604-092601-3729-496.txt`)

**Mechanism.** `rbtdrk_role_invest_impl` divests-then-invests for both roles
specifically to make standing-depot reruns race-free. The director divest ran
and reported "Divest operation completed" — yet the invest's create-preflight
(which refuses to overwrite an existing SA) immediately saw the SA as still
present. The divest's gone-confirmation and the create-preflight read a
not-yet-converged view of the same SA. A *state check minutes later* showed
`director-canest-dir` fully gone, with **no** soft-delete reservation
(`--show-deleted` count 0) — i.e. the deletion was real; the create-preflight
simply read stale. The retriever, divested+recreated in the same run, won the
same race; the director lost it.

This is the same eventual-consistency class as Face 2, on a different edge
(delete→read rather than create→validate).

## Why it presented as a macOS problem (it isn't)

DRS validation and SA delete/create propagation are server-side and
host-independent. canonical-invest passes routinely on cerebro and
`cygwin@rocket` because those runs won the races; the two macOS runs lost one
race each on consecutive days. There is **no macOS-specific defect** in either
face. The macOS crucible itself is sound: `siege` (local kludge build + tether
charge + full ifrit sortie set) is 60/60 green on macOS Docker Desktop,
including `conntrack_spoofed_ack` (which native Linux passes and Windows DD
breaches — a separate ₣BV finding).

## Fix direction

**Federation dissolves all three.** The roadmap's operator-federation tier
(RBSHR; `memo-20260527-operator-credential-models.md`) grants capabilities to
stable external-IdP `principal://` subjects with revocation central at the
depot. There is no per-cycle SA create/delete/grant — so no binding to leak
(Face 1), no fresh SA to fail DRS validation (Face 2), and no delete/create
edge to race (Face 3). The three faces are all artifacts of churning a
short-lived SA every cycle; the federation model simply does not churn.

**Keyfile-tier stopgaps** (interim; retire when federation lands or when the
keyfile tier is deprecated):

- *Face 2:* add the DRS org-policy 400 (`"incompatible with the provided
  policy"` / `"do not belong to a permitted customer"`) to
  `rbgi_add_project_iam_role`'s propagation-retry tolerance, with a deadline
  matching Google's documented window (minutes; budget ≥7). This is the
  smallest fix that unblocks `blockade`/`skirmish`/`dogfight` reliably.
- *Face 3:* harden the divest→invest handoff so the create-preflight tolerates
  a not-yet-propagated prior deletion (retry the "already exists" preflight, or
  have the create share the divest's gone-confirmation path) rather than failing
  hard.
- *Face 1:* either make divest revoke bindings before deleting the SA (mirror
  the grant), or — better aligned with federation — stop churning: invest once
  on a **stable** identity and **rotate the key** instead of delete-and-recreate
  (the governor's datestamped-per-mantle shape is the worst offender and the
  clearest candidate to make stable). Independently, a one-time sweep of the
  existing 145 project tombstones (+ GAR repo + Mason SA) clears the current
  pollution; it is safe (touches only already-deleted principals) and was shown
  writable by Experiment A.

The principle (CMK "load-bearing complexity" / the Pale): DRS and GCP eventual
consistency are a neighbor's realm — we conduct ourselves against them
(characterize, retry the surveyed signature, log the bend) rather than legislate
them. The stopgaps above are membranes with a retirement condition
(federation), not permanent structure.

## Open / secondary

- The per-role asymmetry within a single run (retriever grant/recreate succeeds
  while director fails) is fully consistent with intermittent races but was not
  independently proven per-role; it needs no further chase unless a
  *deterministic* per-role difference reappears.
- Whether DRS evaluates the whole submitted policy or only the changed members
  was not settled directly; Experiment A (whole tombstoned policy resubmits
  clean) shows that, whichever it is, existing `deleted:` members are not the
  trigger.

## Evidence appendix

- DRS / policy state: `gcloud resource-manager org-policies …`,
  `gcloud projects get-iam-policy cancbhm-d-canest3bhm100001` (payor-token
  authenticated, 2026-06-04).
- Experiment A: `set-iam-policy` of the unchanged 145-tombstone policy → 200.
- Experiment B′: `add-iam-policy-binding … roles/cloudbuild.builds.editor` to
  the day-old director SA → 200.
- Face 2 original: `logs-buk/hist-rbw-adI-sh-20260603-103842-26773-301.txt`.
- Face 3 rerun: `logs-buk/hist-rbw-adI-sh-20260604-092730-7113-822.txt`,
  `logs-buk/hist-rbw-ts-dogfight-20260604-092601-3729-496.txt`.
- Code: `rbgi_add_project_iam_role` and the accepted-tombstone comment
  (`Tools/rbk/rbgi_IAM.sh`); `zrbgg_divest_role`, `rbgg_invest_director`,
  `rbgg_invest_retriever` (`Tools/rbk/rbgg_Governor.sh`);
  `rbtdrk_role_invest_impl` (`Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrk_canonical.rs`).
- Google docs: Restrict identities with domain-restricted sharing; Troubleshoot
  organization policy errors for service accounts.

[restricting-domains]: https://cloud.google.com/resource-manager/docs/organization-policy/restricting-domains
[troubleshoot-org-policies]: https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/troubleshoot-org-policies

It captures the phenomenon as **one root (per-cycle SA churn) with three faces**:
- **Face 1** — the binding tombstone leak (mechanism in code, blast radius across 3 resources, Experiment A proving it's benign-but-unbounded toward the ~1500-member cap).
- **Face 2** — the create→grant DRS race that blocked `blockade` (Google-documented, Experiment B′ confirms, `rbgi` retry-tolerance gap).
- **Face 3** — the delete→create race that blocked `dogfight` (state check confirms the SA really deleted; create-preflight read stale).

Plus: why it presented as macOS but isn't; **federation as the unifying dissolver** with keyfile-tier stopgaps that each carry a retirement condition; an honest open/secondary section for the nuances we didn't fully close; and an evidence appendix with the durable log paths, experiments, and code symbols.

It's written to match the heat's finding-memo house style (title + dated/heat preamble + Headline). A couple of judgment calls I made you may want to weigh:
- I framed federation as *the* fix and the keyfile patches explicitly as stopgaps-to-retire — leaning on your "the fix must account for federation" steer. If you'd rather the memo stay neutral on direction, say so.
- I included the line-number-free code symbols rather than `file:line`, so it won't rot.

content.text:
First — you're right, and I over-rotated. Let me drop "federation is the fix." It's the *successor tier*, not the MVP repair; the current model stays and must be repaired in place.

**The name:** the roadmap (RBSHR) calls the current model the **keyfile tier** — each role holds an RBRA service-account key file, possession of the key *is* the identity, no org required. (Artifact = RBRA, spec RBSRA; the two-tier plan is in `memo-20260527-operator-credential-models.md`.) So the repair we want is "fix the leak and races *within* the keyfile tier." Federation just happens to also dissolve them later — I'll demote it to a footnote in the memo, not the fix.

## The one fact that organizes your three ideas

**An IAM binding names a specific principal** (`serviceAccount:director-canest-dir@…?uid=N`). That single fact is what your (1)/(2)/(3) are circling, and it splits cleanly into two repair *shapes*:

**Repair A — keep churning, but clean up (your idea 1).** Keep creating a fresh SA each cycle; make divest *fully* tear down: revoke its bindings on all three resources (project + GAR repo + Mason SA) **before** deleting the SA, with a propagation wait.
- Fixes **Face 1** (leak) and **Face 3** (delete→create race).
- Does **not** fix **Face 2** — every cycle still mints a *fresh* SA that must be granted and can still lose the DRS recognition race. You'd still need the retry-tolerance patch.

**Repair B — stop churning (your ideas 2 + 3 together).** Establish a **stable** SA per role-type, granted its roles **once** ("the profile"), at depot establishment. Re-credentialing then means **rotate the key on the stable SA** — not delete-and-recreate the SA.
- Fixes **all three faces**: no new principal → nothing to re-grant (no tombstone), nothing fresh for DRS to fail to recognize, no delete/create edge to race.
- Still pure keyfile tier — the operator still receives a fresh RBRA; the only change is that the *principal and its bindings* persist while the *key* rotates. To the operator, "re-key by recreate" and "re-key by key-rotation" are indistinguishable; the difference is entirely server-side.

So your (3) — "refer to the global profile instead of making copies" — only *works* because of that one fact: you can reuse a grant only if the principal it names is stable. That's Repair B, and it's the more complete one. (1) is the lesser repair that treats the symptom of churn rather than removing it.

They're not exclusive: even under B you still want a *real* teardown (your idea 1) for genuinely retiring an identity — just not on every re-key.

## What I don't understand yet — and it decides A vs B

I won't pretend to know the design intent. Three things I'd need to ground before recommending:

1. **Why does the keyfile tier churn at all?** The code calls it "re-key" but re-keys by *recreating* the SA. Was recreate chosen deliberately (e.g., a blunt guarantee the old key is dead), or is it incidental? If key-rotation gives the same guarantee, B is nearly free.
2. **Why is the governor datestamped** (`governor-YYYYMMDDHHMM`, a genuinely new principal per mantle) when director/retriever reuse a fixed name? That asymmetry suggests an intentional governor-rotation ceremony I don't yet grasp.
3. **Is a role-type's SA meant to be shared or per-operator-identity?** The `director-<identity>` shape implies per-identity. That decides what "global profile" even means — one stable SA per type, or one per identity granted once.

## Proposal

Before we commit either repair, let me ground these in the specs — `RBSGM` (governor mantle), `RBSDK`/`RBSRK` (director/retriever invest), `RBSRA` (credential format), `RBSDE`/`RBSDN` (depot levy/init), and the two credential-model memos — and come back with the design intent behind the churn. *Then* A-vs-B is a real decision instead of my guess.

And I'll re-frame the drafted memo: keyfile-tier repair primary (with A/B laid out as above), federation demoted to "eventual successor tier that also happens to dissolve these."

Want me to go read those specs now and report the churn rationale, or keep reasoning it through with you first?

3.  Chuckle, I clearly don't recall the vocabulary here.  Can you re-ask in simpler terms? content.text:
Yes — that split is exactly the right simplification, and the 100 tombstones are precisely the symptom of *not* having made it. Let me put it in plain terms.

## Re-asking my question 3, simply

When two different operators (two people, or two machines) each need to act as a **director** on the same depot, which is intended:

- **(a) one shared director identity** — there's a single `director` account, and each operator just holds a copy of its key, or
- **(b) one director identity per operator** — `director-alice`, `director-bob`, each its own account?

I ask because the logs show a *single* fixed `director-canest-dir` being recreated over and over, which looks like (a): one director identity per depot, re-keyed each time. If that's the intent, "establish once" means *once per depot*. If it's really (b), "establish once" means *once per operator*. Either way the split works — it only changes whether the one-time step lives in depot-setup or in operator-enrollment.

## The split, in three buckets

Today, **every re-key does all of this**: make the account → grant its permissions → make a key. That's why permissions pile up — you rebuild the whole identity when you only needed a new key. Separate them:

1. **One-time (the "profile" — stable, never churns):** create the account **and grant its permissions, once.** The account + its permission grants *are* the profile. This is the part that should live in depot setup (or operator enrollment) and never run again.

2. **Per-RBSA (the only thing that should churn):** **rotate the key.** Delete the old key, make a new key → hand out the new RBRA. Touches *only keys* — never the account, never the permissions.

3. **Rare — true teardown:** when you genuinely retire an identity for good, *then* revoke the permissions and delete the account (your "stronger delete," done once at end-of-life, not every cycle).

## Your "blunt kill of the old key" survives intact

That was the whole reason for delete-and-recreate, and you don't lose it. You can delete a **key** directly — the account stays, the old key is dead. Even bluntly: delete *all* keys, then create one, so only the new key can possibly work. Same guarantee you have today, without destroying the identity to get it.

## Why this kills all three problems

- **Tombstone leak:** permissions are granted once and never re-granted → nothing to leave behind. (Today: re-granted every cycle, never revoked → 145 dead entries.)
- **Fresh-account DRS race:** no new account per cycle → nothing for the domain policy to fail to recognize.
- **Delete→recreate race:** the account never gets deleted on a re-key → no "already exists" race.

So the pile of tombstones is the receipt for the conflation: re-keying tore down and rebuilt the identity when it should have just swapped the key.

## On the governor (your #2)

The governor is the low-churn, deliberately-simplified one — one at a time, rarely used, ~30 dead owners accrued slowly over weeks. The split *would* help it too (a stable governor, re-keyed), but it's not the urgent part; **director and retriever are** (69 + 46 tombstones, churned every suite run). I'd leave the single-governor simplification alone and aim the repair at director/retriever first — unless you want governor folded in.

Does (a)-vs-(b) land for you? Once you tell me which, I think we have the repair shape clearly enough to write it down — and I'll fold this three-bucket framing into the memo as the keyfile-tier repair.

content: Goal: fix the IAM credential leak by splitting one-time identity setup from per-key rotation. We've agreed the repair shape. Next: you answer whether directors are one shared identity per depot or one per operator, so I can write it into the memo. (disable recaps in /config)

content.text:
**Objects**
- **SA** — the GCP account, e.g. `director-canest-dir`. Identity = email **+ uid** (recreating the same name = new uid).
- **Key** — credential material *on* an SA. Serialized to disk as the **RBRA** file the operator holds.
- **Binding** — one `(role → member)` entry; the member *is* an SA; it lives on a **Resource**.
- **Resource** — a thing that carries a policy: the **project**, the **GAR repo**, the **Mason SA**.

**Three independent lifecycles**
1. **SA**: create → exists → delete. *Delete leaves its Bindings behind as `deleted:` ghosts.*
2. **Key**: add → valid → delete. *Delete = blunt kill of that credential; SA untouched.*
3. **Binding**: grant (add member) → active → **revoke (remove member)**. Keyed by member = SA.

**What re-credentialing actually needs:** Key lifecycle only — add new Key, delete old Key.

**The hole (two parts)**
- **H1 — Binding lifecycle is half-wired.** `grant` runs at invest; `revoke` is **never called**. Divest deletes the SA and stops. GCP can't drop a binding whose member vanished, so it ghosts it. → grants `+1`/cycle, revokes `0` → unbounded.
- **H2 — wrong lifecycle invoked for re-key.** "Re-key" runs the **SA** lifecycle (delete+recreate) when it only needed the **Key** lifecycle (rotate). So the SA *and its Bindings* churn every time, when only the Key had to.

**Why both exist:** the code fused "issue a credential" with "create the identity." One operation does SA-create + Binding-grant + Key-create together, and its inverse only does SA-delete. The Binding-revoke and the standalone Key-rotate were never separated out.

**Fix in one line:** move grant/revoke to run **once per SA** (create / true-teardown); make re-key run **only the Key lifecycle**. Then Bindings stop churning and the ghosts stop.

message.content: That... is an extremely clear statement.  Double check your 'fix in one line' concept: is it that simple?  Do you want to reframe the memo given this precise understanding?

content.text:
The concept is correct and complete. But "one line" undersells the doing — it's a small **refactor + a one-time sweep**, not a one-liner. The honest checklist:

1. **It's a split, not a line.** The fused invest becomes two operations: **establish** (ensure SA + ensure Bindings) and **re-key** (rotate Key only). One verb → two.
2. **Establish must be idempotent** — "ensure," not "create." Safe to re-run; a no-op when already established. (This is also what kills the "already exists" race.)
3. **Re-key presumes established.** Strictly it's *assert SA+Bindings present, then rotate Key*. If absent → establish first.
4. **Blunt-kill preserved:** re-key = delete *all* Keys, add one. SA and Bindings untouched. (Your #1 guarantee, intact.)
5. **The DRS fresh-SA race doesn't fully vanish — it relocates.** It can still bite *at establish* (a fresh SA, freshly granted). But establish now runs ~once per identity instead of every cycle, so keep the retry-tolerance guard there as cheap insurance; it's just off the hot path now.
6. **Existing ghosts still need a one-time sweep.** The split stops *new* tombstones; it doesn't clean the 145 + 23 + 23 already there.
7. **Cross-host see-saw is orthogonal — unchanged.** A blunt re-key invalidates other holders of a shared identity either way; the split neither fixes nor worsens it.
8. **Governor stays as-is** (your call — low churn). Fix scoped to director/retriever.

So: idea that simple; execution = split invest into establish/re-key + sweep + keep the one guard. Small, but more than a line.

## Reframe the memo — yes

The current draft buries the mechanism under "three faces / federation dissolves it." This understanding is sharper. New skeleton:

1. **Objects** — SA, Key, RBRA, Binding, Resource.
2. **Three lifecycles** — SA / Key / Binding (with their proper inverses).
3. **The hole** — H1 (Binding lifecycle half-wired: grant, never revoke) + H2 (re-key runs the SA lifecycle, not the Key lifecycle). *This is the spine.*
4. **The three faces as consequences** — leak ← H1; both races ← H2's churn.
5. **The fix** — lifecycle split (establish-once + re-key), with the 8 checklist items as the real scope.
6. **Federation** — one-line footnote: the eventual tier that happens to obviate this; not the MVP fix.

Want me to rewrite the memo on that skeleton now?

input.content:
# Credential lifecycle: the Binding-revoke hole (keyfile tier)

*2026-06-04. Surfaced in heat ₣BV while bringing the `blockade` (moriah / airgap)
crucible up on macOS Docker Desktop (branch `bv-macos-crucible-20260603-BVAAE`);
repair tracked under `rbk-08-credential-repairs`. Depot under study: the
canonical standing depot `cancbhm-d-canest3bhm100001`, org `247899326218`.*

## Headline

The keyfile credential tier (RBSHR's name for the current model: each role holds
an RBRA key file, possession = identity) **fuses three independent object
lifecycles into one operation**, and never wires the revoke half of one of them.
The result: IAM bindings accumulate without bound, and re-credentialing trips two
separate Google eventual-consistency edges. All of it follows from one structural
hole, stated precisely below. None of it is macOS-specific.

## Objects

- **SA** — a GCP service account, e.g. `director-canest-dir@…`. Identity is
  email **+ uid**; recreating the same name yields a *new* uid.
- **Key** — credential material *on* an SA. Serialized to disk as the **RBRA**
  file the operator holds. (Key = GCP-side; RBRA = its delivery.)
- **Binding** — one `(role → member)` entry; the member *is* an SA; it lives on
  a **Resource**.
- **Resource** — a thing carrying an IAM policy: the **project**, the **GAR
  repo**, the **Mason SA**.

## Three independent lifecycles

1. **SA**: create → exists → delete. *On delete, GCP cannot drop bindings whose
   member vanished — it retags them `deleted:…?uid=` and keeps them.*
2. **Key**: add → valid → delete. *Deleting a Key is a blunt kill of one
   credential; the SA is untouched.*
3. **Binding**: grant (add member) → active → **revoke (remove member)**. Keyed
   by member = SA.

Re-credentialing an operator *requires only the Key lifecycle*: add a new Key,
kill the old one.

## The hole

The code fuses "create the identity" with "issue a credential." One invest does
**SA-create + Binding-grant + Key-create** together; its only inverse (divest)
does **SA-delete** alone. From that fusion, two defects:

- **H1 — the Binding lifecycle is half-wired.** `grant` runs at invest;
  **`revoke` is never called anywhere.** Divest deletes the SA and stops
  (`zrbgg_divest_role` never opens an IAM policy). So Bindings are `+1` per
  invest and `0` per divest — monotonic, unbounded.
- **H2 — re-key invokes the wrong lifecycle.** "Re-key" runs the **SA**
  lifecycle (delete + recreate — chosen as a blunt guarantee the old Key is
  dead) when it only needed the **Key** lifecycle. So the SA *and its Bindings*
  churn every issuance, when only the Key had to.

## The three faces (all consequences of the hole)

- **Binding leak ← H1.** Bindings granted, never revoked, accumulate as
  `deleted:` ghosts on every Resource. Observed: **project 145** (69 director,
  46 retriever, 30 governor-owner), **GAR repo 23**, **Mason SA 23**. *Benign so
  far — Experiment A (below) shows the 145-ghost policy still resubmits cleanly —
  but it marches toward GCP's ~1500-member allow-policy cap independent of any
  org policy.* A comment in `rbgi_add_project_iam_role` already records the
  ghosts as a known, "accepted" cost; they have now become unbounded.
- **create→grant race ← H2.** Each cycle mints a *fresh* SA and immediately
  grants it. The org enforces Domain Restricted Sharing
  (`iam.allowedPolicyMemberDomains`, allow-listing customer `C02ystilf` =
  scaleinvariant.org); a brand-new SA is not yet recognized as in-customer, so
  the grant is rejected (HTTP 400, "incompatible with the provided policy"), and
  `rbgi`'s retry tolerance does **not** cover that error — so it dies on the
  first try. *This blocked `blockade`.* Google documents the propagation lag
  (≈2 min, up to 7+). [restricting-domains], [troubleshoot-org-policies]
- **delete→recreate race ← H2.** Divest deletes the SA and reports it gone, but
  the immediate re-create's preflight still sees it ("already exists"). *This
  blocked `dogfight`* the next morning. A state check minutes later showed the SA
  fully gone with no soft-delete reservation — the create-preflight simply read
  a not-yet-converged view.

Both races are **intermittent and host-independent** (server-side); they pass
routinely on cerebro/Cygwin and lost on macOS two days running. The macOS
crucible itself is sound — `siege` is 60/60 green there.

## The fix — separate the lifecycles

Stop fusing. Split the one invest into two operations:

- **Establish** (one-time per SA — at depot setup / operator enrollment):
  ensure the SA exists and **grant its Bindings once**. Idempotent — a no-op when
  already established.
- **Re-key** (every issuance): **rotate the Key only** — delete all existing
  Keys (the blunt kill, preserved), add one. Never touches the SA or its
  Bindings.
- **Teardown** (rare — genuine retirement): the missing inverse — **revoke
  Bindings, then delete the SA** (H1's never-wired half), run once at end-of-life
  rather than every cycle.

This closes H1 (Bindings granted once, revoked on real teardown — never churned)
and H2 (re-key is Key-only; the SA never gets recreated). Scope notes:

1. It's a refactor, not a line: one fused verb → establish / re-key (+ a real
   teardown).
2. Establish must be **idempotent** ("ensure," not "create").
3. Re-key **presumes established** (assert SA + Bindings present, else establish).
4. Blunt-kill preserved: re-key = delete *all* Keys, add one.
5. The create→grant DRS race doesn't vanish — it **relocates to establish**
   (now ≈once per identity, off the hot path). Keep the DRS-400 in `rbgi`'s
   retry tolerance there as cheap insurance.
6. Existing ghosts (145 + 23 + 23) need a **one-time sweep** — the split stops
   new ones, doesn't clean old. Safe: only `deleted:*` removed (Experiment A
   shows the policy is writable).
7. Cross-host see-saw is **orthogonal** — a shared identity re-keyed bluntly
   invalidates other holders either way; unchanged.
8. **Governor out of scope** (deliberate single-governor, datestamped,
   low-churn simplification). Fix targets director/retriever.

## Footnote: federation

The roadmap's operator-federation tier (RBSHR; `memo-20260527-operator-credential-models.md`)
grants capabilities to stable `principal://` subjects with central revocation —
no per-cycle SA at all, so it would obviate this hole entirely. It is the
**eventual successor tier, not the MVP fix**; the keyfile tier stays and is
repaired in place as above.

## Evidence appendix

- DRS + policy state: `gcloud resource-manager org-policies …`,
  `gcloud projects get-iam-policy cancbhm-d-canest3bhm100001` (payor-token, 06-04).
  DRS allow-list = `C02ystilf`.
- **Experiment A** — `set-iam-policy` of the unchanged 145-ghost project policy
  → HTTP 200. Ghosts do not block grants.
- **Experiment B′** — grant `roles/cloudbuild.builds.editor` to the now-propagated
  director SA → HTTP 200. Freshness was the only variable.
- create→grant race: `logs-buk/hist-rbw-adI-sh-20260603-103842-26773-301.txt`.
- delete→recreate race: `logs-buk/hist-rbw-adI-sh-20260604-092730-7113-822.txt`,
  suite `logs-buk/hist-rbw-ts-dogfight-20260604-092601-3729-496.txt`.
- Code: `rbgi_add_project_iam_role` + the accepted-ghost comment
  (`Tools/rbk/rbgi_IAM.sh`); `zrbgg_divest_role`, `rbgg_invest_director`,
  `rbgg_invest_retriever` (`Tools/rbk/rbgg_Governor.sh`);
  `rbtdrk_role_invest_impl` (`Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrk_canonical.rs`).

[troubleshoot-org-policies]: https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/troubleshoot-org-policies

# Credential lifecycle: the Binding-revoke hole (keyfile tier)

*2026-06-04. Surfaced in heat ₣BV while bringing the `blockade` (moriah / airgap)
crucible up on macOS Docker Desktop (branch `bv-macos-crucible-20260603-BVAAE`);
repair tracked under `rbk-08-credential-repairs`. Depot under study: the
canonical standing depot `cancbhm-d-canest3bhm100001`, org `247899326218`.*

## Headline

The keyfile credential tier (RBSHR's name for the current model: each role holds
an RBRA key file, possession = identity) **fuses three independent object
lifecycles into one operation**, and never wires the revoke half of one of them.
The result: IAM bindings accumulate without bound, and re-credentialing trips two
separate Google eventual-consistency edges. All of it follows from one structural
hole, stated precisely below. None of it is macOS-specific.

## Objects

- **SA** — a GCP service account, e.g. `director-canest-dir@…`. Identity is
  email **+ uid**; recreating the same name yields a *new* uid.
- **Key** — credential material *on* an SA. Serialized to disk as the **RBRA**
  file the operator holds. (Key = GCP-side; RBRA = its delivery.)
- **Binding** — one `(role → member)` entry; the member *is* an SA; it lives on
  a **Resource**.
- **Resource** — a thing carrying an IAM policy: the **project**, the **GAR
  repo**, the **Mason SA**.

## Three independent lifecycles

1. **SA**: create → exists → delete. *On delete, GCP cannot drop bindings whose
   member vanished — it retags them `deleted:…?uid=` and keeps them.*
2. **Key**: add → valid → delete. *Deleting a Key is a blunt kill of one
   credential; the SA is untouched.*
3. **Binding**: grant (add member) → active → **revoke (remove member)**. Keyed
   by member = SA.

Re-credentialing an operator *requires only the Key lifecycle*: add a new Key,
kill the old one.

## The hole

The code fuses "create the identity" with "issue a credential." One invest does
**SA-create + Binding-grant + Key-create** together; its only inverse (divest)
does **SA-delete** alone. From that fusion, two defects:

- **H1 — the Binding lifecycle is half-wired.** `grant` runs at invest;
  **`revoke` is never called anywhere.** Divest deletes the SA and stops
  (`zrbgg_divest_role` never opens an IAM policy). So Bindings are `+1` per
  invest and `0` per divest — monotonic, unbounded.
- **H2 — re-key invokes the wrong lifecycle.** "Re-key" runs the **SA**
  lifecycle (delete + recreate — chosen as a blunt guarantee the old Key is
  dead) when it only needed the **Key** lifecycle. So the SA *and its Bindings*
  churn every issuance, when only the Key had to.

## The three faces (all consequences of the hole)

- **Binding leak ← H1.** Bindings granted, never revoked, accumulate as
  `deleted:` ghosts on every Resource. Observed: **project 145** (69 director,
  46 retriever, 30 governor-owner), **GAR repo 23**, **Mason SA 23**. *Benign so
  far — Experiment A (below) shows the 145-ghost policy still resubmits cleanly —
  but it marches toward GCP's ~1500-member allow-policy cap independent of any
  org policy.* A comment in `rbgi_add_project_iam_role` already records the
  ghosts as a known, "accepted" cost; they have now become unbounded.
- **create→grant race ← H2.** Each cycle mints a *fresh* SA and immediately
  grants it. The org enforces Domain Restricted Sharing
  (`iam.allowedPolicyMemberDomains`, allow-listing customer `C02ystilf` =
  scaleinvariant.org); a brand-new SA is not yet recognized as in-customer, so
  the grant is rejected (HTTP 400, "incompatible with the provided policy"), and
  `rbgi`'s retry tolerance does **not** cover that error — so it dies on the
  first try. *This blocked `blockade`.* Google documents the propagation lag
  (≈2 min, up to 7+). [restricting-domains], [troubleshoot-org-policies]
- **delete→recreate race ← H2.** Divest deletes the SA and reports it gone, but
  the immediate re-create's preflight still sees it ("already exists"). *This
  blocked `dogfight`* the next morning. A state check minutes later showed the SA
  fully gone with no soft-delete reservation — the create-preflight simply read
  a not-yet-converged view.

Both races are **intermittent and host-independent** (server-side); they pass
routinely on cerebro/Cygwin and lost on macOS two days running. The macOS
crucible itself is sound — `siege` is 60/60 green there.

## The fix — separate the lifecycles

Stop fusing. Split the one invest into two operations:

- **Establish** (one-time per SA — at depot setup / operator enrollment):
  ensure the SA exists and **grant its Bindings once**. Idempotent — a no-op when
  already established.
- **Re-key** (every issuance): **rotate the Key only** — delete all existing
  Keys (the blunt kill, preserved), add one. Never touches the SA or its
  Bindings.
- **Teardown** (rare — genuine retirement): the missing inverse — **revoke
  Bindings, then delete the SA** (H1's never-wired half), run once at end-of-life
  rather than every cycle.

This closes H1 (Bindings granted once, revoked on real teardown — never churned)
and H2 (re-key is Key-only; the SA never gets recreated). Scope notes:

1. It's a refactor, not a line: one fused verb → establish / re-key (+ a real
   teardown).
2. Establish must be **idempotent** ("ensure," not "create").
3. Re-key **presumes established** (assert SA + Bindings present, else establish).
4. Blunt-kill preserved: re-key = delete *all* Keys, add one.
5. The create→grant DRS race doesn't vanish — it **relocates to establish**
   (now ≈once per identity, off the hot path). Keep the DRS-400 in `rbgi`'s
   retry tolerance there as cheap insurance.
6. Existing ghosts (145 + 23 + 23) need a **one-time sweep** — the split stops
   new ones, doesn't clean old. Safe: only `deleted:*` removed (Experiment A
   shows the policy is writable).
7. Cross-host see-saw is **orthogonal** — a shared identity re-keyed bluntly
   invalidates other holders either way; unchanged.
8. **Governor out of scope** (deliberate single-governor, datestamped,
   low-churn simplification). Fix targets director/retriever.

## Footnote: federation

The roadmap's operator-federation tier (RBSHR; `memo-20260527-operator-credential-models.md`)
grants capabilities to stable `principal://` subjects with central revocation —
no per-cycle SA at all, so it would obviate this hole entirely. It is the
**eventual successor tier, not the MVP fix**; the keyfile tier stays and is
repaired in place as above.

## Evidence appendix

- DRS + policy state: `gcloud resource-manager org-policies …`,
  `gcloud projects get-iam-policy cancbhm-d-canest3bhm100001` (payor-token, 06-04).
  DRS allow-list = `C02ystilf`.
- **Experiment A** — `set-iam-policy` of the unchanged 145-ghost project policy
  → HTTP 200. Ghosts do not block grants.
- **Experiment B′** — grant `roles/cloudbuild.builds.editor` to the now-propagated
  director SA → HTTP 200. Freshness was the only variable.
- create→grant race: `logs-buk/hist-rbw-adI-sh-20260603-103842-26773-301.txt`.
- delete→recreate race: `logs-buk/hist-rbw-adI-sh-20260604-092730-7113-822.txt`,
  suite `logs-buk/hist-rbw-ts-dogfight-20260604-092601-3729-496.txt`.
- Code: `rbgi_add_project_iam_role` + the accepted-ghost comment
  (`Tools/rbk/rbgi_IAM.sh`); `zrbgg_divest_role`, `rbgg_invest_director`,
  `rbgg_invest_retriever` (`Tools/rbk/rbgg_Governor.sh`);
  `rbtdrk_role_invest_impl` (`Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrk_canonical.rs`).

[restricting-domains]: https://cloud.google.com/resource-manager/docs/organization-policy/restricting-domains
[troubleshoot-org-policies]: https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/troubleshoot-org-policies
