-## Face 3 — the delete→create race (blocked `dogfight`)
-**What happened (06-04).** Re-running the real path via `dogfight`
-(canonical-invest + dogfight): governor-mantle ✓, retriever-invest ✓,
-director-invest failed — but this time *before* any grant:
-ERROR: Service account director-canest-dir@… already exists —
-       run the matching GovernorDivests verb (rbw-adD) first to re-key
-(durable: `logs-buk/hist-rbw-adI-sh-20260604-092730-7113-822.txt`;
-suite `logs-buk/hist-rbw-ts-dogfight-20260604-092601-3729-496.txt`)
-**Mechanism.** `rbtdrk_role_invest_impl` divests-then-invests for both roles
-specifically to make standing-depot reruns race-free. The director divest ran
-and reported "Divest operation completed" — yet the invest's create-preflight
-(which refuses to overwrite an existing SA) immediately saw the SA as still
-present. The divest's gone-confirmation and the create-preflight read a
-not-yet-converged view of the same SA. A *state check minutes later* showed
-`director-canest-dir` fully gone, with **no** soft-delete reservation
-(`--show-deleted` count 0) — i.e. the deletion was real; the create-preflight
-simply read stale. The retriever, divested+recreated in the same run, won the
-same race; the director lost it.
-This is the same eventual-consistency class as Face 2, on a different edge
-(delete→read rather than create→validate).
-## Why it presented as a macOS problem (it isn't)
-DRS validation and SA delete/create propagation are server-side and
-host-independent. canonical-invest passes routinely on cerebro and
-`cygwin@rocket` because those runs won the races; the two macOS runs lost one
-race each on consecutive days. There is **no macOS-specific defect** in either
-face. The macOS crucible itself is sound: `siege` (local kludge build + tether
-charge + full ifrit sortie set) is 60/60 green on macOS Docker Desktop,
-including `conntrack_spoofed_ack` (which native Linux passes and Windows DD
-breaches — a separate ₣BV finding).
-## Fix direction
-**Federation dissolves all three.** The roadmap's operator-federation tier
-(RBSHR; `memo-20260527-operator-credential-models.md`) grants capabilities to
-stable external-IdP `principal://` subjects with revocation central at the
-depot. There is no per-cycle SA create/delete/grant — so no binding to leak
-(Face 1), no fresh SA to fail DRS validation (Face 2), and no delete/create
-edge to race (Face 3). The three faces are all artifacts of churning a
-short-lived SA every cycle; the federation model simply does not churn.
-**Keyfile-tier stopgaps** (interim; retire when federation lands or when the
-keyfile tier is deprecated):
-- *Face 2:* add the DRS org-policy 400 (`"incompatible with the provided
-  policy"` / `"do not belong to a permitted customer"`) to
-  `rbgi_add_project_iam_role`'s propagation-retry tolerance, with a deadline
-  matching Google's documented window (minutes; budget ≥7). This is the
-  smallest fix that unblocks `blockade`/`skirmish`/`dogfight` reliably.
-- *Face 3:* harden the divest→invest handoff so the create-preflight tolerates
-  a not-yet-propagated prior deletion (retry the "already exists" preflight, or
-  have the create share the divest's gone-confirmation path) rather than failing
-  hard.
-- *Face 1:* either make divest revoke bindings before deleting the SA (mirror
-  the grant), or — better aligned with federation — stop churning: invest once
-  on a **stable** identity and **rotate the key** instead of delete-and-recreate
-  (the governor's datestamped-per-mantle shape is the worst offender and the
-  clearest candidate to make stable). Independently, a one-time sweep of the
-  existing 145 project tombstones (+ GAR repo + Mason SA) clears the current
-  pollution; it is safe (touches only already-deleted principals) and was shown
-  writable by Experiment A.
-The principle (CMK "load-bearing complexity" / the Pale): DRS and GCP eventual
-consistency are a neighbor's realm — we conduct ourselves against them
-(characterize, retry the surveyed signature, log the bend) rather than legislate
-them. The stopgaps above are membranes with a retirement condition
-(federation), not permanent structure.
-## Open / secondary
-- The per-role asymmetry within a single run (retriever grant/recreate succeeds
-  while director fails) is fully consistent with intermittent races but was not
-  independently proven per-role; it needs no further chase unless a
-  *deterministic* per-role difference reappears.
-- Whether DRS evaluates the whole submitted policy or only the changed members
-  was not settled directly; Experiment A (whole tombstoned policy resubmits
-  clean) shows that, whichever it is, existing `deleted:` members are not the
-  trigger.
## Evidence appendix
-- DRS / policy state: `gcloud resource-manager org-policies …`,
-  `gcloud projects get-iam-policy cancbhm-d-canest3bhm100001` (payor-token
-  authenticated, 2026-06-04).
-- Experiment A: `set-iam-policy` of the unchanged 145-tombstone policy → 200.
-- Experiment B′: `add-iam-policy-binding … roles/cloudbuild.builds.editor` to
-  the day-old director SA → 200.
-- Face 2 original: `logs-buk/hist-rbw-adI-sh-20260603-103842-26773-301.txt`.
-- Face 3 rerun: `logs-buk/hist-rbw-adI-sh-20260604-092730-7113-822.txt`,
-  `logs-buk/hist-rbw-ts-dogfight-20260604-092601-3729-496.txt`.
-- Code: `rbgi_add_project_iam_role` and the accepted-tombstone comment
+- DRS + policy state: `gcloud resource-manager org-policies …`,
+  `gcloud projects get-iam-policy cancbhm-d-canest3bhm100001` (payor-token, 06-04).
+  DRS allow-list = `C02ystilf`.
+- **Experiment A** — `set-iam-policy` of the unchanged 145-ghost project policy
+  → HTTP 200. Ghosts do not block grants.
+- **Experiment B′** — grant `roles/cloudbuild.builds.editor` to the now-propagated

+- create→grant race: `logs-buk/hist-rbw-adI-sh-20260603-103842-26773-301.txt`.

+- Code: `rbgi_add_project_iam_role` + the accepted-ghost comment
(`Tools/rbk/rbgi_IAM.sh`); `zrbgg_divest_role`, `rbgg_invest_director`,
`rbgg_invest_retriever` (`Tools/rbk/rbgg_Governor.sh`);
`rbtdrk_role_invest_impl` (`Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrk_canonical.rs`).

Memo rewritten on the skeleton — spine is now Objects → 3 lifecycles → **H1 (Binding lifecycle half-wired) + H2 (re-key runs the SA lifecycle, not the Key lifecycle)** → the three faces as consequences → the establish/re-key/teardown split → federation demoted to a footnote.

Now your two decisions:

**1) Clear the tombstones with gcloud — can I, should we?**
- **Can:** yes. Per Resource (project, GAR repo, Mason SA): GET policy → drop every `deleted:*` member (and any binding left empty) → set-iam-policy. Experiment A already proved the cleaned policy is accepted. Low risk — only already-dead principals removed; live members + Google agents preserved. I'd show you the exact removed-vs-kept diff before writing.
- **But it's hygiene, not the unblock.** Experiment A proved ghosts don't block grants. What actually blocks continuity is that the director SA is **gone** (dogfight deleted it) — you need it *re-established*, which is the race-prone invest, and *pre-fix that re-establish re-adds a few ghosts*. So the sweep is a clean reset; the real continuity step is re-establishing the director (and retrying through the DRS race if it bites).
- **Rec:** for today, re-establish the director (continuity); sweep is optional now and could just be the heat's first pace. Happy to do both on your word.

**2) Per-person, multi-role RBRA (one file per *person*, any role combo) — good idea?**
- **Yes — endorse, as a consider-pace after the fix.** It collapses the duplicated role-typed verbs, aligns the keyfile tier with federation's *principal → capability-set* shape (smoother eventual migration), and one-file-per-person is clearer ownership. The lifecycle split is its *enabler*: once establish (identity) and re-key (credential) are separated, "a principal with a role-set" is the natural next shape.
- **The tension the pace must resolve:** today's role-typed SAs enforce least-privilege *by construction* (a retriever literally cannot act as director). A per-person multi-role SA is only as least-privilege as the grants you pick — so the pace must show it *preserves* that guarantee, not just the convenience.

**On your go**, I'll: nominate **₣rbk-08-credential-repairs** (racing) → slate **pace 1 = the lifecycle-split fix** (citing this memo) → **pace 2 = consider per-person multi-role RBRA**. And separately run the **sweep + director re-establish** for today's continuity (showing you the sweep diff first). Say go — or adjust the pace shape.

input.content:
# jjezs_slate lifecycle-split-establish-rekey

## Character
Intricate but mechanical refactor; the one judgment is the establish/re-key boundary.

Close the credential-lifecycle hole on director + retriever. The fused invest does
SA-create + Binding-grant + Key-create, and its only inverse (divest) does SA-delete
alone — so Bindings are never revoked (H1) and re-key churns the whole SA (H2).

Split the fused invest into three operations:
- establish (idempotent — ensure SA exists + grant its Bindings once)
- re-key (Key-only — delete all Keys, add one; the blunt-kill guarantee preserved)
- teardown (revoke Bindings, then delete SA — the never-wired inverse; only at genuine retirement)

Source: `Memos/memo-20260604-credential-churn-leak-and-propagation-races.md` — objects,
the three lifecycles, H1/H2, and the 8 scope notes (idempotent establish; re-key presumes
established; keep the DRS-400 in rbgi's retry tolerance at establish; governor out of scope).

Done: re-key touches only Keys; Bindings granted once and revoked only on teardown;
standing-depot reruns stop accruing `deleted:` tombstones.

# jjezs_slate consider-per-person-multirole-rbra

## Character
Design exploration — judgment, not mechanical. Output is a recommendation, not an implementation.

Evaluate refactoring RBRA from one-file-per-role-type (director, retriever — each with its
own cult verbs) to one-file-per-person carrying any combination of roles. Collapses the
role-typed verb surface and aligns the keyfile tier with the federation model's
principal -> capability-set shape (smoother eventual migration).

The lifecycle split is its enabler: once identity (establish) and credential (re-key) are
separated, "a principal with a role-set" is the natural next shape.

Key question to resolve: does per-person multi-role preserve least-privilege? Today's
role-typed SAs enforce it by construction — a retriever cannot act as director. A per-person
SA is only as least-privilege as the grants chosen; show the security guarantee survives,
not just the convenience.

Source: the federation footnote in `Memos/memo-20260604-credential-churn-leak-and-propagation-races.md`;
`Memos/memo-20260527-operator-credential-models.md`.

Done: a decision/recommendation (do / don't / how), not implementation.

aiTitle: Review BV mount and git commit history

content.text:
Read-out before I touch main:

**Nothing to notch** — tree is clean. And the merge is genuinely clean: **no divergence anywhere.** Local main is 0 ahead of the branch, origin/main is 0 ahead, everything fast-forwards linearly. The gallops conflict you feared **isn't present** — no competing heat edits on main to collide with.

**What a whole-branch FF lands on main** (9 commits, 5 files):

| Group | files |
|---|---|
| **₣BY credential work** (clearly wanted) | `.claude/jjm/jjg_gallops.json`, `jjp_uBuY.md` (₣BY paddock), the memo |
| **₢BVAAE blockade edit** (partial — now known incomplete) | `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrc_crucible.rs` |
| **macOS kludge-tadmor stamps** (host-local digests) | `rbmm_moorings/tadmor/rbrn.env` |

The one thing worth your eye: the bottom two are ₣BV macOS *exploration* artifacts. The `tadmor/rbrn.env` stamps point at image digests built on *this* macOS daemon — on main, another host couldn't pull them until it re-kludges (though if tadmor stamps are accepted per-host churn, like the cygwin pollution, this is normal).

**The trap to avoid:** the "surgical" alternative — cherry-pick only the ₣BY commits to main, leave the macOS artifacts on the branch — would split the gallops JSON between branch and main and **re-introduce exactly the heat-in-branch divergence you wanted to dodge.** So surgical is the *messier* path here, not the cleaner one.

**My recommendation:** fast-forward the whole branch to main (clean, linear, no gallops mess), accept the macOS stamps as normal kludge churn that the next kludge overwrites. Then optionally push (origin/main is 10 behind HEAD, also a clean FF).

Want me to FF the whole branch to main? And push, or leave the push to you?

message.content: Check main again, I had forgotten to push from parallel chats.  Retry.  Then give me a terse prompt to start a chat with the pace we originally started with- do include directions to create a new branch though.

content.text: Re-checking against the now-pushed origin/main:

input.command:
git fetch origin --quiet 2>&1 | tail -3
echo "HEAD ahead of origin/main: $(git rev-list --count origin/main..HEAD)"
echo "origin/main ahead of HEAD: $(git rev-list --count HEAD..origin/main)"
echo "local main ahead of origin/main: $(git rev-list --count origin/main..main)  | origin/main ahead of local main: $(git rev-list --count main..origin/main)"
echo "=== origin/main commits NOT on HEAD (the parallel work) ==="
git log --oneline HEAD..origin/main | head -40
echo "=== do those parallel commits touch the gallops JSON? (conflict risk) ==="
git log --oneline HEAD..origin/main -- .claude/jjm/jjg_gallops.json | head
echo "=== files the parallel work changed (origin/main vs merge-base) ==="
git diff --stat $(git merge-base HEAD origin/main)..origin/main | tail -40

```
Mount ₣BV pace ₢BVAAE (requal-cerebro-and-wsl). Before any work: git fetch, then
create a fresh feature branch off origin/main named with today's date + the coronet
(e.g. bv-requal-20260604-BVAAE). Do NOT create heats on this branch. Then orient on

conntrack_spoofed_ack PASS); blockade is blocked by a credential-lifecycle bug now
carried in its own heat (rbk-08-credential-repairs); the WSL leg is the likely
remaining gap for this pace.
```

Want me to re-home ₣BY onto a fresh branch off origin/main now (preserving the heat + memo), or leave that to you?

content.text:
From the branch reflog — authoritative:

```
4a7e54ded  bv-macos-crucible-20260603-BVAAE@{10}: branch: Created from HEAD
```

**The branch was created on `4a7e54ded`** ("OFFICIUM 260603-1000") — but that's just a jjx_open commit with no code change over its parent. The actual safe baseline you're remembering is its parent:

**`1afc3fcf5`** — *"Captured the pre-refactor green baseline on cerebro … Full skirmish (11 fixtures: 252 passed / 0 failed / 1 skipped) … the frozen before-state … Passed on cerebro."*

That green cerebro baseline is the safe place. `4a7e54ded` is code-identical to it (officium dir only), so for branching purposes the two are interchangeable.

One caveat if you're thinking of reusing it as a base: `1afc3fcf5` is now **~44 commits behind current main** (the parallel ₣BH/₣BX/₣BD Lode + foundry work, plus our credential merge all landed since). It's still a known-green point, but a fresh branch off it would be missing all of that. For new windows-issue work you'd more likely want current `main` (or `1afc3fcf5` only if you specifically want that frozen baseline isolated from the churn).

message.content: You noted that we ended up with duplicate officia due to working on two different machines.  Create a prompt I can issue in a clean chat to slate a spook in BD to address this- the pace should prompt us to design a fix together- I am not clear how we should fix and I don't want to think about it until we mount that pace.

content.text:
Here's the prompt — it slates the spook with a docket that *frames* the design without pre-deciding it, and tells the instance to slate-and-stop:

```
Slate a spook (a new pace) in heat ₣BD (jjk-v4-1-fast-tweaks) capturing this JJK
infrastructure stumble. Just slate it — do NOT start designing the fix; the docket
deliberately sets up a design-it-together conversation for whenever we mount the pace.

Silks: officium-id-collision-cross-machine

Docket:
## Character
Design conversation — the fix is undecided; mount to design it together.

Spook (team-infrastructure stumble): jjx_open mints officium ids that are
time-bucketed (YYMMDD-HHMM), so two machines that call jjx_open within the same
minute mint the SAME id. Observed 2026-06-04: ☉260604-1000 was minted independently
on two hosts — one landed on origin/main as commit be145c9b7 ("OFFICIUM 260604-1000"),
another in a parallel chat on a different machine — both writing
.claude/jjm/officia/260604-1000/. That time the gallops automerge reconciled it by
luck and jjx_validate passed, but the collision defeats the opaque/unique intent of
officium ids and is a latent hazard for the officium directory and its gazette
exchange files under concurrent multi-machine work.

Done: an agreed design (not yet chosen) for making officium ids collision-safe across
concurrent machines.
```

The clean chat will open its own officium, write that to `gazette_in.md`, and `jjx_enroll {firemark: "BD"}` — appending the spook to ₣BD. It won't design anything; the docket's `## Character` line and open-ended `Done` reserve that for when you mount it.
